this section, we review a selection of publications most relevant to our work, and
introduce the notion of queue imbalance, which forms the basis for our empirical
study.
3.1 Zero-Intelligence Models of LOB State
Early studies of the links between queue dynamics and price formation in an
LOB typically assumed that order ows were governed by simple, independent
stochastic processes. Several authors postulated models of LOB state to for-
mulate predictions of future price movements. Models of this type are often
called \zero-intelligence" LOB models, because they are not motivated by ra-
tional traders who seek to achieve speci ed trading goals. Smith et al. [2003]
introduced a zero-intelligence model in which limit order arrivals, market order
arrivals, and cancellations all occur as mutually independent Poisson processes
with  xed rate parameters. Cont et al. [2010] extended this model by allow-
ing the rates of limit order arrivals and cancellations to vary across prices.
Huang et al. [2015] studied a model in which the order arrival rates depend
on the lengths of order queues in L(t). Mike and Farmer [2008] considered a
zero-intelligence framework in which order ows also exhibit long-range auto-
correlations, in agreement with empirical data.
Despite the apparent simplicity of these approaches, zero-intelligence LOB
models suer from several important drawbacks. First, the LOB dynamics that
emerge from their interacting order ows are often extremely complex. Conse-
quently, most analysis of zero-intelligence LOB models relies heavily on exten-
6

sive simulations, rather than analytical treatment. Second, although they make
reasonably good predictions of the long-run statistical properties of LOB state
(see, e.g., Farmer et al. [2005]), zero-intelligence LOB models typically produce
poor predictions of price movements. Third, because such models ignore the
strategies implemented by real traders, they fail to capture many important
correlations and feedback loops between dierent order ows. This weakness
further harms their ability to make good predictions of future price movements
in real markets.
3.2 Simplifying the State Space
Motivated by these weaknesses, several authors have proposed models that seek
to produce more realistic LOB dynamics on a simpli ed LOB state space. Cont
and De Larrard [2013] introduced a model in which nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are
assumed to be governed by independent diusion processes. By studying this
model in the hydrodynamic limit, in which stochastic uctuations are dominated
by deterministic ows, the authors obtained analytical expressions for several
quantities of interest, such as the distribution of times between price changes,
the distribution and autocorrelation of price changes, and the probability of a
mid-price movement in a given direction, given nb(bt;t) andna(at;t).
Despite the appealing nature of these results, Gar eche et al. [2013] presented
empirical results to illustrate that the dynamics of nb(bt;t) andna(at;t) are
strongly inuenced by a coupling between the two queues, and thereby brought
into question Cont and de Larrard's assumption of independence between the
dynamics of nb(bt;t) andna(at;t). Avellaneda et al. [2011] studied a similar
model in which nb(bt;t) andna(at;t) are also governed by diusion processes,
but with a speci ed correlation . The authors solved their model to deduce a
simple, closed-form predictor for the direction of the next mid-price movement,
givennb(bt;t),na(at;t), and. Although their assumption that nb(bt;t) and
na(at;t) are related by a simple correlation parameter is clearly a simpli ca-
tion of reality, the authors argued that their model produces reasonably good
predictions of price movements in real LOBs.
3.3 Queue Imbalance in an LOB
In a recent publication, Yang and Zhu [2015] argued that the property of the
best bid and ask queues most useful for price prediction is not their lengths, but
rather their imbalance. Speci cally, at a given time t, let
I(t) :=nb(bt;t)�na(at;t)
nb(bt;t) +na(at;t)(7)
denote the queue imbalance at time t. The quantity Imeasures the (normalized)
dierence between nb(bt;t) andna(at;t), and thereby provides a quantitative
assessment of the relative strengths of buying and selling pressure in an LOB.2

IfI0 (which occurs when nb(bt;t) andna(at;t) are approximately equal),
then the buying and selling pressures are approximately balanced. If I > 0
(which occurs when nb(bt;t)>na(bt;t)), then the bid queue is longer than the
ask queue, which suggests that there is a net positive buying pressure in the
LOB. Conversely, if I <0 (which occurs when nb(bt;t)<na(bt;t)), then the ask

selling pressure in the LOB. Values of Iclose to 1 suggest a very strong buying
pressure, and values of Iclose to�1 suggest a very strong selling pressure.
Empirical study of how the queue imbalance in an LOB aects future price
changes dates back almost a decade, to when Cao et al. [2009] studied how
the value of Iat a speci ed depth inside the LOB (i.e., not only at the best
quotes) inuenced mid-price returns during the subsequent 5-minute interval.
Stoikov and Waeber [2015] noted that the optimal execution of a single order
over a short time horizon depends on the queue imbalance in an LOB. Cartea
et al. [2015] studied queue imbalance at the trade-by-trade level on Nasdaq,
and noted that the rate of buy (respectively, sell) market order arrivals and the
probability of observing an upward (respectively, downward) price movement
both increase considerably when Iis strongly positive (respectively, strongly
negative). Based on their analysis, they designed a trading algorithm whose
objective is to place both buy and sell limit orders and thereby generate pro ts
from round-trip trades.
Similarly to these previous publications, the aim of the present paper is to
investigate whether queue imbalance Iinuences the future price dynamics in
an LOB. Speci cally, we seek to complement the analyses of queue imbalance
in these papers with a more formal, quantitative analysis of whether the queue
imbalanceIin an LOB provides signi cant predictive power for the direction of
the next mid-price movement. In contrast to the previous work in this area, we
do not restrict our attention to large-tick stocks, but rather study the predictive
power of queue imbalance for a selection of both small-tick and large-tick stocks.
By comparing our results from these dierent stocks, we seek to illuminate
how the underlying market microstructure underpins the usefulness of Ias a
predictor of future price movements.
4 Data
The data that we study originates from the LOBSTER database, which pro-
vides an event-by-event description of the temporal evolution of the LOB for
each stock listed on Nasdaq.3The LOBSTER database contains very detailed
information regarding the temporal evolution of the relevant LOBs. However,
for the present study we require only the time series of bid prices b(t), ask prices
a(t), and queue lengths nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t). We derive all of the statistics
that we use for our empirical analysis from these 4 simple time series. To pro-
choose to use nb(bt; t)�na(at; t) as the numerator because it instead produces an imbalance
on the interval [�1;1], and thereby simpli es sign conventions.
3For a detailed introduction to LOBSTER, see http://LOBSTER.wiwi.hu-berlin.de .
8

duce our empirical results in Section 6, we study these time series for a selection
of 10 liquid stocks during the entire year of 2014.
The Nasdaq platform operates continuous trading from 09:30 to 16:00 on
each weekday. Trading does not occur on weekends or public holidays, so we
exclude these days from our analysis. We also exclude all activity during the
 rst and last 30 minutes of each trading day, to ensure that our results are
not aected by the abnormal trading behaviour that can occur shortly after
the opening auction or shortly before the closing auction. After making these
exclusions, we therefore study all trading activity from 10:00 to 15:30 on each
of the 252 trading days in 2014.
On the Nasdaq platform, each stock is traded in a separate LOB with price{
time priority, with a tick size of = $0:01 (see Section 2). Although this tick
size is common to all stocks, the prices of dierent stocks on Nasdaq vary across
several orders of magnitude (from about $1 to more than $1000). Therefore,
therelative tick size (i.e., the ratio between the stock price and ) varies con-
siderably across dierent stocks.
In order to facilitate comparisons between stocks with dierent relative tick
sizes, we  rst ranked all stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange according to their
total dollar volume of trades during 2014. From this list, we then selected the
top 5 entries whose maximal trade price was below $50 :00, and the top 5 entries
whose minimal trade price was above $100 :00 (see Table 1). We call the  rst
group of stocks large-tick stocks because their low price makes their relative tick
size large, and we call the second group of stocks small-tick stocks because their
high price makes their relative tick size small. We also repeated our calculations
for several other stocks whose prices fell between these two thresholds. We found
that some such stocks behaved similarly to the large-tick stocks in our sample,
whereas other behaved similarly to the small-tick stocks in our sample. In order
to illustrate the clear separation between the results for dierent types of stocks,
we present only our results for the large-tick and small-tick stocks in our sample,
and not for the other stocks with intermediate tick size. Table 1 lists the names
of the stocks that we choose in this way, along with several summary statistics
that describe their aggregate market activity.
The LOBSTER data has many important bene ts that make it particu-
larly suitable for our study. First, the data is recorded directly by the Nasdaq
servers. Therefore, we avoid the many diculties associated with data sets that
are recorded by third-party providers, such as misaligned time stamps or incor-
rectly ordered events. Second, the data is fully self-consistent, in the sense that
it does not report any activities or updates that would violate the standard rules
of LOB trading. By contrast, many other LOB data sets suer from recording
errors that can constitute a considerable source of noise when performing de-
tailed analysis. Third, each limit order described in the data constitutes a  rm
commitment to trade. Therefore, our results reect the market dynamics for
real trading opportunities, not \indicative" declarations of possible intent.
The LOBSTER database describes all LOB activity that occurs on Nasdaq,
but does not provide any information regarding order ow for the same assets on
dierent platforms. To minimize the possible impact on our results, we restrict

5 Methodology
5.1 Sample Construction
For each stock and each trading day in our sample, we  rst create an ordered
setTof times at which the mid price changes,

We choose to study price changes via this simple indicator variable, rather than
studying the signed change in mid price, because the magnitude of such price
changes are determined not only by nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t), but also by the
prices and lengths of order queues deeper into the LOB. We therefore restrict
our attention to the direction of mid-price movements, not their size.
For each time ti2T, we choose a time ~tiuniformly at random in the

Section 8.
For all stocks in our sample, both the number and temporal spacing between
LOB events that aect nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) (i.e., market order arrivals and
limit order arrivals and cancellations at the best quotes) varies considerably
across dierent time intervals ( ti�1;ti). In some cases, the arrivals of such
11

events are highly clustered, in the sense that the time intervals contain some
periods with no updates to nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) and other periods with
many updates to nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t). In order to understand whether
this event clustering strongly inuences our results, we also repeated all of our
calculations when constructing our random sample in event time . To do so, for
each timeti2T, we  rst construct a list of the times that either nb(b(t);t)
orna(a(t);t) changed, during the time interval ( ti�1;ti). We then choose our
sampling time ~tiuniformly at random from this (discrete) set of event times.
We found that all of our empirical results when choosing ~tiin this way were
qualitatively the same as those that we report throughout the paper, for which
we choose ~tiuniformly at random in the open interval ( ti�1;ti).
5.2 In-Sample and Out-of-Sample Data
The number of mid-price changes that occur in a single trading day varies
considerably both across dierent stocks and across dierent days. To ensure
that our sample contains the same number of data points for each stock each
day, we therefore draw a random subsample with a  xed size among the N
possible choices in the set T. For the results that we show in Section 6, we
use a random subsample size4of 100. For each stock, we then aggregate the
subsamples from each of the 252 dierent trading days to produce an aggregated
data set of 25200 data points.
A key contribution of the present work is assessing the strength of predictive
power provided by I. To avoid the possible dangers of data snooping when
performing this analysis, we randomly partition each stock's aggregated data
set into two disjoint subsets: a training set, which contains 80% of the data (i.e.,
20160 data points), and a testing set, which contains 20% of the data (i.e., 5040
data points). We perform the  ts of our logistic regressions and local logistic
regressions using the training set (i.e., \in sample"), then evaluate the predictive
power of these  ts using the testing set (i.e., \out of sample").

5.4 Assessing Predictions
To assess the predictive power of our logistic regressions for performing binary
and probabilistic classi cation, we compare their output to that of a simple null

In words, our null model predicts that the probability of an upward price move-
ment is always 1 =2, irrespective of the queue imbalance.
To assess the predictive power of our  ts for performing binary classi cation,
we calculate the out-of-sample receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves
and the corresponding area-under-ROC-curve statistics. The area under the

imbalanceIi0chosen uniformly at random among all observations for which

observations for which yi= 0, the area under the ROC curve is equal to the
probability that the resulting values of ^ ywill satisfy ^ yi0>^yj0. For a detailed
introduction to ROC curves, see Bradley [1997] and Hanley and McNeil [1982].
To assess the predictive power of our  tted logistic regressions for performing

5.5 Local Logistic Regression
After performing our logistic regression  ts, we also consider an alternative es-
timation of the function ^ yby performing a local logistic regression of yionto
Ii. A local logistic regression is a semi-parametric estimation method that  ts a
separate logistic regression at each point in the domain. Speci cally, for a given
imbalanceI, the local logistic regression estimator of ^ y(I) is obtained by per-
forming a standard logistic regression at I, but weighting the input observations
according to their distance from I. For a detailed introduction to local logistic
regression, see Loader [2006]. When performing our local logistic regression  ts,
we use a standard tricube weight function with a nearest-neighbour bandwidth
parameter, whose value we choose by performing a 5-fold cross validation in
the training set. For a detailed discussion of parameter estimation via cross
validation, see Hastie et al. [2010].

analyze the resulting curves.

Third, the  tted values of x1are much larger for large-tick stocks (for which
they vary from about 2 to about 3) than for small-tick stocks (for which they
vary from about 0 :5 to about 0 :8). These dierences in parameter estimates sim-
ilarly produce substantial dierences in the logistic regression  ts. For large-tick
stocks, the large values of x1produce substantial curvature in the  tted logistic
regression curves (see the left panel of Figure 4), whereas for small-tick stocks,
the small values of x1produce much atter, shallower  tted logistic regression
curves (see the right panel of Figure 4). Together, these results suggest that
strong imbalances (of either sign) lead to stronger levels of predictability for
large-tick stocks than they do for small-tick stocks. For example, the logistic
regression curves predict that when Iis close to 1, the probability of an upward
price move is about 0 :8 to 0:9 for large-tick stocks, but only about 0 :6 to 0:7 for
small-tick stocks.
We next turn to the question of whether the x0andx1coecients cause
a statistically signi cant impact in the output of the logistic regressions. To
address this question, we perform a Wald test for the x0andx1coecients in
turn (see the  rst two columns of Table 3).
At the 95% level, the intercept x0is not statistically signi cant for MSFT,
INTC, MU, AMZN, TSLA, PCLN, or NFLX, but is statistically signi cant for
CSCO, ORCL, and GOOG. For CSCO and ORCL, x0is also statistically signi -
cant at the 99% level. This result suggests that there is a statistically signi cant
asymmetry between buy-side and sell-side activity for these stocks. We stress,
18

however, that the strength of this asymmetry is very weak (see Table 2), and
that we are only able to detect it with statistical signi cance because our sam-
ple is large. Therefore, even for these stocks, the behaviour of the  tted logistic
regression curve is very close to symmetric about I= 0.
The coecient x1is statistically signi cant at the 99% level for all 10 stocks
in our sample. This implies that the logistic regressions detect a strongly sta-
tistically signi cant relationship between the queue imbalance and the direction
of the subsequent mid-price movement.
We next turn to the question of the statistical signi cance of the full logistic
regression  ts. To address this question, we perform a likelihood ratio test of the
 tted logistic regressions against a nested model that contains only the intercept
term, and thereby excludes the possible inuence of queue imbalance (see the
third column of Table 3). For all 10 stocks in our sample, the results of the
likelihood ratio test are statistically signi cant at the 99% level, which allows
us to conclude with high statistical con dence that the relationship between ^ y
andIillustrated by our logistic regressions is highly statistically signi cant.
6.3 Assessing Predictive Power
In Section 6.2, we concluded from our logistic regressions that the relation-
ship between the queue imbalance and the direction of the subsequent mid-
price movement is highly statistically signi cant. We now turn to the question
of how strongly the estimated logistic regression curve ^ yimproves the out-of-
sample performance of binary and probabilistic classi cation, in comparison to
a simple null model, which assumes that the direction of mid-price changes is
uncorrelated with the queue imbalance I. Due to the large size of our sample, it
could be the case that ^ yproduces a relatively small increase in predictive power,
despite the logistic regression  ts being statistically signi cant.
We  rst address the out-of-sample performance of our logistic regression  ts
for performing binary classi cation. To do so, we calculate the ROC curves (see
Section 5.3) for each of our logistic regression  ts in Section 6.2. We show these
ROC curves, together with the corresponding ROC curve for the null model, in
Figure 5.
In each case, the out-of-sample ROC curves lie above the grey line for all
choices of speci city, which implies that the logistic regression  ts outperform
the out-of-sample predictive power of the null model at all levels of speci city.
In Table 4, we list the area under the ROC curve for each stock (see Section
5.4).
For large-tick stocks, the area under the ROC curve varies from about 0 :7
to about 0:8. For small-tick stocks, the results are weaker, and vary from about
0:6 to about 0 :65. In both cases, however, these results indicate that Iprovides
a substantial improvement in the out-of-sample predictive power of the binary
classi er. To verify that these results are not inuenced by over- tting, we also
calculate the area under the corresponding ROC curves for the in-sample  ts.
In each case, the in-sample values of the statistics in Table 4 are very similar

We next address the out-of-sample predictive power of our probabilistic clas-
si er. To do so, we compute the mean squared residual ri(see Equation (19))
between the observed value of yiand the predicted probability ^ yi, across all
observations in the testing set (see Table 5).
For large-tick stocks, the mean squared residuals vary from about 0 :18 to
about 0:2. For small-tick stocks, the mean squared residuals vary from about
0:235 to about 0 :245. For the null model, the mean squared residual of the null
model is exactly 1 =4 (see Section 5.4). Therefore, when compared to the null
model, the logistic regression  ts provide a reduction in mean squared residual
of about 20% to 30% for large-tick stocks, and about 2% to 6% for small-tick
stocks. For all stocks, the in-sample values of the mean squared residuals are
very similar to the corresponding out-of-sample values, which con rms that the
logistic regressions do not suer from over- tting.
The results in Tables 4 and 5 together present an interesting picture of the
out-of-sample performance of our logistic regression  ts. In terms of binary
classi cation of the direction of price movements (which we measure by calcu-
lating the area under the ROC curve), the logistic regression  ts perform well for
large-tick stocks and reasonably well for small-tick stocks. In terms of predicting
the probability of an upwards mid-price movement (which we measure by cal-
culating the mean squared residuals), the logistic regression  ts again perform
well for large-tick stocks. For small-tick stocks, however, the results are much
weaker, and the logistic regression  ts only slightly outperform the null model.
Therefore, despite the queue imbalance being a statistically signi cant predictor
of subsequent price movements for all stocks, the improvement in out-of-sample
forecasting power that it provides varies considerably across stocks.
21

6.4 Local Logistic Regressions
In Sections 6.2 and 6.3, we calculated logistic regression  ts to estimate a para-
metric relationship between the queue imbalance and the direction of the sub-
sequent mid-price movement. Although these results are useful for perform fast
and simple calculations regarding the statistical signi cance of the possible rela-
tionship between Iandy, the parametric nature of this approach could obscure
the detailed market dynamics that underpin this relationship, because the shape
of the  tted logistic regression curves is constrained by the parametric form of
the logistic sigmoid function in Equation (17).
To help address this problem, we now complement our results in Sections 6.2
and 6.3 with a semi-parametric approach, by  tting local logistic regression
curves to the same data (see Section 5.3). In contrast to the logistic regression
 ts, these semi-parametric  ts enable us to consider more carefully the subtle
relationship between queue imbalance and mid-price movements, and thereby
help to illuminate the market dynamics that underpin our results.
Figure 6 shows our  tted local logistic regression curves for each of the
stocks in our sample. For each  t, we use a tricube weight function with a
nearest-neighbour bandwidth parameter. To choose this bandwidth parameter,
we perform a 5-fold cross validation within our training set, using the mean
squared residual ri(which we seek to minimize) as our objective function. Al-
though the globally optimal choice of bandwidth parameter varies somewhat
across the dierent stocks in our sample, in all cases it resides between about
0:5 and about 0 :8. For the results that we present in this section, we use the
bandwidth parameter 0 :65. We also repeated all of our calculations for a range
of dierent bandwidth choices between 0 :6 and about 0 :7, and we found that
our results were qualitatively similar for all choices in this range.
For large-tick stocks, the local logistic regression curves are approximately
monotone increasing functions of I, which suggests that larger values of Iicor-
respond to larger values of yi. Similarly to the logistic regression curves (see
Figure 4), the local logistic regressions predict that the probability of an up-
ward price movement is about 0 :8 to 0:9 whenIis close to 1. In contrast to
the logistic regression curves, however, the local logistic regression curves sug-
gest that the behaviour of the system exhibits 2 dierent regimes. For values
ofIbetween about�0:25 and about 0 :25, the ^ycurve is quite steep, which
indicates that when the bid and ask queues have similar lengths, a relatively
small dierence in the queue imbalance corresponds to a considerable change
in the probability that the next price movement will be upwards. Outside of
this region, the steepness of the ^ ycurve decreases considerably. Therefore, for
values ofIless than about�0:25 or greater than about 0 :25, a further dierence
in queue imbalance corresponds to a smaller change in the probability that the
next price movement will be upwards.
For small-tick stocks, the local logistic regression curves predict that the
probability of an upward price movement is about 0 :6 whenIis close to 1. For
all small-tick stocks except PCLN, the local logistic regression curves are non-
monotonic in I. This result is rather puzzling, because it suggests that there

7 Discussion
Our results in Section 6 illustrate the existence of a statistically signi cant rela-
tionship between the queue imbalance and the direction of the subsequent mid-
price movement. For large-tick stocks, the relationship depends quite strongly
on the queue imbalance (see upper rows of Table 2) and provides a considerable
improvement in out-of-sample predictive power in terms of both binary classi -
cation (see Table 4) and probabilistic classi cation (see Table 5). For small-tick
stocks, the relationship depends less strongly on the queue imbalance (see lower
rows of Table 2), and the improvement in out-of-sample predictive power is more
moderate (see Table 6).
Among the stocks in our sample, the weakest out-of-sample performance that
we observe occurs in the probabilistic classi cation for GOOG, for which our
 ts outperform the null model by about 2%. Although this number is certainly
small compared to the performance that we achieve for other stocks, it is impor-
tant to remember that many practitioners invest huge sums of money to improve
their trading strategies by tiny fractions of a percentage point. Therefore, even
this very moderate performance for GOOG could be economically signi cant
for some market participants. Moreover, both of our performance measures
are unconditional averages that make forecasts for all data points, many of
which correspond to situations where the imbalance is small (see Figure 2), and
therefore where the predictability of the mid-price movement is weak. If we con-
sidered only the situations in which the queue imbalance was close to 1, then
the out-of-sample performance of our estimators would improve considerably.
This observation is particularly important from a practical standpoint, because
some practitioners may only be interested in forecasting when the ability to do
so is likely to be strong, and may therefore simply abstain from trading when
I0.
Similarly, it seems reasonable to assume that Iis less informative when
bothnb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are small, because the arrival of a single buy
(respectively, sell) market order is likely to cause the mid price to increase
(respectively, decrease). Therefore, if we considered only the situations in which
bothnb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are considerably larger than 0, then the out-of-
sample performance of our estimators would again improve considerably.
It is interesting to consider why our results for large-tick stocks are so dif-
25

ferent from our results for small-tick stocks. We believe that the answer to this
puzzle lies in the underlying market microstructure. Recall from Section 2 that
in an LOB, there are usually two ways for m(t) to change: by a new limit order
arriving inside the bid{ask spread, or by one of the bid or ask queue lengths
depleting to 0. However, the mean bid{ask spread for the large-tick stocks in
our sample is very close to its minimum possible value of the platform's tick size,
s(t) == $0:01 (see Table 1). This behaviour has an important consequence
for LOB dynamics, because it removes the possibility that a new limit order will
arrive inside the bid{ask spread, and thereby eliminates one of the two possible
reasons for changes in m(t). It is therefore reasonable to believe that the queue
imbalance (which quanti es the relative lengths of the bid and ask queues) will
provide stronger predictive power when s(t) =, because the probability of an
upwards price movement is governed only by the probability that the ask queue
depletes before the bid queue (which, in turn, depends directly on the queue
lengths), and not on the probability that a new buy limit order arrives inside
the spread.
Even when s(t)> , there are still strong reasons for why market partici-
pants may behave dierently for small-tick and large-tick stocks. Similarly to
most other LOBs, the Nasdaq platform operates a price{time priority rule (see
Section 4), by which priority is given to the active buy (respectively, sell) orders
with the highest (respectively, lowest) price, and ties are broken by selecting
the active order with the earliest submission time. When s(t)>, any market
participant has the opportunity to submit a buy (respectively, sell) limit order
with higher priority than any others in the LOB, simply by choosing the price of
this order to be one tick higher than b(t) (respectively, lower than a(t)). There-
fore, the tick size determines the cost of \buying" priority in the LOB. For
large-tick stocks, this cost is relatively high, so many traders choose to submit
new limit orders that wait in the queues at the best quotes, and the typical
sizes ofnb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are large. For small-tick stocks, this cost is
relatively low, so many traders choose to submit new limit orders inside the
bid{ask spread, and the typical sizes of nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are small. As
noted above, the predictive power of Iis likely to be larger in situations where
nb(b(t);t) andna(a(t);t) are larger. In this way, the stronger out-of-sample per-
formance for large-tick stocks can similarly be attributed to the longer queue
lengths that typically occur for these stocks.
For some stocks in our sample, we  nd a weak but statistically signi cant
asymmetry in the  tted logistic regressions, which manifests as a non-zero value
of the intercept x0(see Table 2). For these stocks, this result suggests that even
when the imbalance is slightly less than 0, the probability that the next mid-
price movement will be upwards is greater than 1 =2. We propose two possible
explanations for this  nding. First, the arrival of exogenous news may cause
traders to change their trading behaviours, irrespective of the queue imbalance.
For example, if a trader receives news that a given company's earnings have
outperformed expectations, then he/she may submit a large market order to
buy the stock (and thereby cause an increase in mid price), even if the current
queue imbalance is negative. We note that all stocks for which we  nd x0to be
26

statistically signi cantly positive underwent considerable price increases during
2014, which is consistent with this hypothesis of an exogenous buying pressure.
Second, some strategic liquidity providers may implement complex strategies
that skew the queue imbalance via an asymmetric submission of limit orders.
For example, if a strategic liquidity provider fears the possibility of a strong
downward price movement, then he/she may choose to submit fewer sell limit
orders than buy limit orders, even in the absence of any information about the
likely future value of the asset. In a recent empirical study of strategic liquidity
provision on Nasdaq, Bonart and Gould [2015] found strong evidence to suggest
that liquidity providers implement strategies that created imbalanced net order
ow at the best quotes. Although it is dicult to test these theories directly, we
believe that both of these explanations are likely to contribute to the behaviour
that we observe.
In addition to our logistic regression  ts, we also perform local logistic re-
gression  ts on the data. By comparing the entries in Tables 4 and 5 to the
corresponding entries in Table 6, it is possible to compare the performance of
the logistic regression and local logistic regression  ts. Most of the entries in
Tables 4 and 5 are equal to the corresponding entries in Table 6, even up to the
third decimal place. This implies that the performance of the two methods is
very similar. In a small number of cases, however, the local logistic regression
 ts slightly outperform the corresponding logistic regression  ts.
Both approaches have bene ts and drawbacks. Performing logistic regression
is much less computationally intensive than performing local logistic regression,
and the full  t of the logistic regression model consists of just 2 scalar values,
x0andx1. Saving the  tted logistic regression curve to a computer hard disk
therefore requires very little storage space. By contrast, saving the  tted local
logistic regression curve requires saving a full copy of the training data, which
can be very large. However, local logistic regression has the important bene t
of providing more detailed information about the underlying LOB dynamics,
because the  tted regression curve is not constrained by the parametric form
of the logistic sigmoid function. Therefore, careful analysis of the local logistic
regression  ts can provide deeper understanding of the results.
8 Conclusions and Outlook
In this paper, we have presented an empirical study of whether the queue im-
balanceIprovides signi cant predictive power for the direction yof the next
mid-price movement. We used data describing the LOB activity for each of 10
liquid stocks on Nasdaq during 2014 to  t logistic regression curves that en-
abled us to perform both binary classi cation and probabilistic classi cation of
y, givenI. For all 10 stocks in our sample, we found that our logistic regressions
identi ed a strongly statistically signi cant relationship between Iandy.
For the large-tick stocks in our sample, our logistic regression  ts provide
a considerable improvement in both binary and probabilistic classi cation. For
the small-tick stocks, we found that the increase in predictive power was more
27

moderate, particularly for probabilistic classi cation. We argued that the reason
for these dierences was the dierences in underlying market microstructure. We
also performed local logistic regression  ts on the same data, and found that
this semi-parametric approach slightly outperforms the logistic regression  ts,
at the expense of being more computationally expensive.
In addition to these practical bene ts, our results also highlight many pos-
sible avenues for future research. Throughout this paper, we have chosen to
measure the queue imbalance via the quantity I, according to Equation (7),
sampled at a time chosen uniformly at random between subsequent changes of
m(t). Although we identify a statistically signi cant relationship between Iand
y, there are many other possible ways to measure queue imbalance in an LOB.
For example, we could measure the value of Iimmediately after or immediately
before each mid-price change, to examine how its predictive power varies accord-
ing to the length of time that elapses before the next price change. Similarly, it
would be interesting to see whether an alternative de nition of the queue imbal-
ance could provide stronger out-of-sample predictive power than the quantity
Ithat we used for this study. Other possibilities could include quantities such
as log(nb(b(t);t)=na(a(t);t)), or even simply nb(b(t);t)�na(a(t);t). Moreover,
we have only studied the imbalance between the best bid and ask queues. It is
possible that the predictive power of our approach could be improved by also
incorporating other statistics about the lengths or imbalance of other queues
deeper into the LOB.
Our results also raise interesting questions about the predictability of price
movements on longer time scales. For example, does Iprovide useful informa-
tion about the direction of price movements further into the future? If so, how
does this predictive power diminish over time? And how do price movements
remain unpredictable on longer timescales, given that we show them to be quite
predictable in our one-step-ahead framework?
Understanding the relationship between queue imbalance and subsequent
price movements is also an important theoretical question. It would therefore
be interesting to build models for how imbalance could evolve over time, and
could thereby aect the subsequent evolution of LOB state. In comparison to
modelling the full state of an LOB (which is an extremely high-dimensional
problem), the simple, one-dimensional nature of I(t) makes modelling its tem-
poral evolution an attractive task. We aim to address these and many other
questions about the predictive power of queue imbalance in our future work.

Abstract
Spoofing is a disruptive order-based manipulation tactic employed by traders to exploit naive
market participants. Spoofers feign interest in trading financial instruments by placing fake or-
ders and creating an illusion of demand and supply that causes the price to deviate from its
fundamental value. This thesis investigates the characteristics of a typical spoof window that
might occur in the limit order book (LOB) of the OMXS30 futures contracts. We modify the
metrics developed by Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) namely High Quoting Activity (HQA), Unbalanced
Quoting (UQ), and Abnormal Cancellations (AC) for identifying potential spoofing using intra-
day LOB data. The contingency table and logistic regression approach are utilized to illustrate
the suitable metrics for identifying potential spoofing and identifying the relationship between
these indicators and the price of the security. LOB data ranging from May to June 2019 is
used with snapshot intervals of 1 second between the two phases of alleged spoofing. A typical
spoof window consists of Phase 1 (Entry) and Phase 2 (Exit). Contingency table results show
a 1-second difference between the cancellation of fake orders and the subsequent placement of
orders on the other side of the LOB. Also, the results indicate that spoofers prefer to place
fake orders on the bid side of the LOB. For the suitability of metrics for identifying potential
spoofing, our results are in line with Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023), however, AC is suggested to be
the weakest indicator among all three metrics. Logistic regression results show UQ features a
negative relationship with the likelihood of an upward movement in the LOB mid-price, whereas
HQA exhibits a positive relationship. For the impact of AC on the mid-price, we obtain in-
significant results. On the whole, spoofing distorts the price-setting mechanism of demand and
supply leading to a degradation of market quality. This study addresses a gap in the existing
literature, which primarily focuses on ex-post study of spoofing, often within the US market. By
investigating ex-ante spoofing in the Swedish context, this research offers novel insights that can
be instrumental for stock market regulators and surveillance analysts.
Keywords: Spoofing, Market Manipulation, Price Discovery, Market Quality, Limit Order Book,
Ex-ante Manipulation

1 Introduction
1.1 Background of the topic
On May 6, 2010, at 2:45 pm, the major stock indices such as the S&P 500, Dow Jones Industrial
Average (DJIA), and the Nasdaq Composite experienced a major decline. Within 10 minutes,
the DJIA decreased substantially by 1,000 points and eventually recouped 600 points in less
than an hour РђЊ the sudden dip amounting to a loss of a trillion dollars adversely affected market
volatility. In2015,theUSDepartmentofJusticeconvictedNavinderSingSarao,ahigh-frequency
trader, for market manipulation. Before the crash, trading from his parentРђЎs London home, Sarao
supposedly placed a large fake order involving the E-mini-S&P 500 futures contracts with the
intentto distort the market and generate profit from his genuine positions. Sarao, the main
culprit responsible for the 2010 РђюFlash CrashРђЮ, had successfully employed spoofing and layering
strategies to distort the market.1The same manipulative strategy was used by JP Morgan in 2020
for US treasury futures and metals, which led the investment bank to get fined $920 million.2
Market Manipulation is defined by the 2003 European Union Market Abuse Directive as an act
where an agent Рђюdisseminates false or misleading information and artificially distorts the price
setting mechanism of financial instrumentsРђЮ. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) states that there is no widely ac-
cepted definition of market manipulation, and its legal interpretations differ across jurisdictions.
Manipulation can be decomposed into four domains: runs, contract-based or benchmark ma-
nipulations, spoofing (order-based), and market power techniques. All forms of manipulation
include placing an order and releasing information to create a false impression of demand and
supply, culminating in an artificial price response of financial security. Not only does market
manipulation induce substantial financial losses, but it also undermines market integrity, reduces
investor confidence, impacts market liquidity, and increases regulatory and compliance costs.
Spoofing is an order-based manipulation strategy defined as Рђюbidding or offering with the intent
tocancel the bid or offer before executionРђЮ.3Putni┼є┼А( 2011)states that the prevalenceof spoofing
in financial markets is troubling for regulators and researchers. Wang ( 2015) identifies spoofing
orders as fake orders submitted by a trader in the market with a dubious intent to mislead other
traders to trade in a specific direction, thereby impeding the price-setting mechanism. A buy
order is placed on the bid side of LOB to indicate buying interest, and a sell order is placed
on the ask side to indicate selling interest. After the favorable price change due to information
asymmetry, spoofers cancel fake orders and execute genuine orders to generate a profit. Do and
Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) label spoof orders as Рђюnon-bonafide ordersРђЮ that are not executed and distort
market price. A spoofing window that begins with a large fakebuy order injects demand-side
interest that induces an upward price revision, which enables the manipulative trader to get a
better price on their sell-side orders and end the spoofing window. On the contrary, when a
spoofer chooses to begin with a large fakesell order, they inject supply-side interest that induces
a downward price revision, enabling them to execute their real buy orders at a lower price.

2https://www.reuters.com/article/jp-morgan-spoofing-penalty-idINKBN26K325
32010 Dodd-F rank Act

Layering is a more complex form of spoofing as it includes placing several spoofing orders to
create the scenario that various traders intend to buy or sell. Brogaard et al. ( 2022) state
that spoofing hinders price discovery by moving prices away from the fundamental value. Price
fluctuations increase the volatility of returns and adverse selection costs. As a result, the bid-ask
spread which refers to the extent by which the ask price exceeds the bid price increases. Hence,
spoofing has serious repercussions for market quality. Their argument aligns with Thel ( 1993)
claims that in some instances, manipulators are price-makers and can generate a profit from
pre-existing contracts contingent on prices like futures contracts of an equity instrument.
We contacted The Swedish Economic Crime Authority and obtained a list of three cases that
prosecuted market participants who had used the spoofing technique to manipulate the Swedish
market. In 2017, the district court indicted a trader who placed large orders worth 58 million
SEK for Oncopeptides, a company listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. The court decided that the
traderРђЎs intent was to never execute the fake orders he placed to influence the equilibrium price.
In the 2019 Economic Crime Authority vs. Syla Skender case, the defendant was found guilty
of buying and selling an equal number of shares of Free2Move Holding AB (FTM) listed on the
Nordic MTF (Multilateral trading facility) with the intent of cancelling his buy orders so he
could sell his shares at a higher price. The court accused Skender of spoofing the MTF to serve
his personal goals. Similarly, in another case in 2019, a trader placed a buy order for 18,000
shares for Omnicar to give the false perception of high demand to execute their sell orders at a
higher price. The court accused the trader of spoofing the market.
Cartea et al. ( 2020) suggests that spoofing and layering are very hard to detect as spoofers can
conceal their true intent and use complex algorithms to avoid getting caught. Fischel and Ross
(1991) posit that it is di№гђicult to prove manipulation as it is based on trader intent that can
be ambiguous. They state that no trade is manipulative unless it gives a false impression of
trading activity. Distinguishing fraudulent intent from legitimate intent increases the burden
of proof for regulators. Placing opposing orders on both sides of the order book could also
constitute normal market activity. Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) outline two unique characteristics of
spoofing by analyzing prosecuted cases from 2010 to 2020; these include high cancellation rates
and substantial order book imbalance. A spooferРђЎs primary intent is to enter orders that they
do not tend to execute to create a false impression of demand and supply.
Brogaard et al. ( 2022) argue that the manipulation of financial markets is an ongoing concern,
exacerbated by automation. High-frequency traders utilize computer programs to execute large
trades within seconds. The agility and accessibility of High-frequency trading enables market
participants to exploit short-term price fluctuations for profit. Numerous studies such as Biais
and Woolley ( 2012) and Leis ( 2012) suggest that high-frequency trading could induce more ma-
nipulation, especially order-based, the likes of which include spoofing. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) enhances
their argument by noting that technological advances have led to an increase in the number of
prosecuted spoofing cases, thus heightening regulatory concerns about such practices.
Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) implies that current literature concentrates on ex-post (after the event) manipu-
lation and its impact on price discovery. Buyers and sellers in a well-functioning financial market
interact with each other to decide the fair price of a financial instrument. This process is called
price discovery. The demand and supply forces equilibrate based on a multitude of factors: mar-
ket sentiment, business cycle stage, and geopolitical conditions. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) suggests that
most researchers can easily trace the aftermath of market manipulation after it has taken place
2

as it is easier to trace price impact in the limit order book after order flow has been disrupted
due to spoofing. Moreover, all prosecuted cases include instances where spoofing has already
occurred in the market, as legally, it becomes easier to prove intent if the damage to the market
can clearly be identified. In contrast, there is a dearth of information on ex-ante manipulation,
specifically on how the potential for manipulation in the market affects liquidity and prices.
Ex-ante spoofing refers to the period preceding manipulation, where traders contemplate how
potential spoofing in the market will change the order flow, liquidity, and price of an instrument
and enable them to generate abnormal profit. Identifying spoofing before it occurs could serve
as a powerful tool for regulators to devise more sound detection systems. Analysts will be able
to detect abnormal patterns in LOB and prevent potential spoofing attacks thereby reducing the
detrimental effects of spoofing on market quality. One of the main gaps in existing research is
the lack of information on how to detect ex-ante spoofing. This creates a pressing need for the
development of more robust indicators of spoofing. Additionally, due to the growth of HFTs,
spoofing is a recurring problem that can lead to market failure as it distorts price discovery.
Ex-ante spoofing poses three dilemmas. Firstly, it is di№гђicult to detect potential spoofing in LOB.
Secondly, it is crucial to decode the dynamics of a spoof window to distinguish spoofing behavior
from normal trading behavior. Thirdly, the identification of suitable spoofing proxies requires
complex empirical analysis. The РђЎspoofing puzzleРђЎ is the amalgamation of all three dilemmas.
The main aim of this paper is to solve the РђЎspoofing puzzleРђЎ by examining different characteris-
tics of a typical spoof window and determining the most suitable indicators to identify ex-ante
spoofing in the limit order book (LOB) in the Swedish futures market. In addition to this, the
paper intends to study the impact of spoofing on price discovery.
1.2 Research question
This research poses three research questions:
1.What are the characteristics of a spoofing window?
2.Which are the relevant metrics for detecting potential spoofing in the Swedish Market?
3.What is the impact of spoofing on price discovery?
We employ a quantitative methodology to study the presence of spoofing behavior in the Swedish
stock market. We begin with studying comprehensive LOB data for OMXS30 futures contracts,
ensuring the availability of a robust dataset for our analysis. Next, we construct spoofing detec-
tion metrics using the LOB data, building upon the methodology established by Do and Putni┼є┼А
(2023) and modifying them to capture the side of the LOB used for potential spoofing. Subse-
quently, we conduct our analysis using a contingency table and logistic regression to model the
relationship between spoofing detection metrics and mid-price movements in the LOB. Finally,
we interpret the results in the context of existing literature and market dynamics, drawing con-
clusions about the characteristics of a spoofing window, suitable metrics for identifying spoofing,
and its impact on the mid-price of the security in the Swedish stock market. This methodological
3

approach allows us to investigate and analyze spoofing behavior, contributing valuable insights 
to the field of market integrit y.
Through detailed analysis of potential spoofing w indows, i ncluding t ime d ifferences between 
order cancellations and subsequent placements on the other side of the LOB, along with the 
identification of a  preferred LOB side for manipulation, we find patterns indicative of  potential 
spoofing activity in the Swedish m arket. Among the designed metrics namely High Quoting 
Activity (HQA), Unbalanced Quoting (UQ), and Abnormal Cancellations (AC), UQ comes out 
as the strongest indicator for detecting potential spoofing. Logistic regression models reveal rela-
tionships between the spoofing detection metrics and price movements, with UQ demonstrating 
expected and significant negative associations with m id-price. W e further use robustness tests 
to validate the significance of the results in identifying and understanding spoofing activities. 
Overall, these findings contribute to a  deeper understanding of the  spoofing dynamics, emphasiz-
ing the importance of specific metrics and their implications for market integrity and regulatory 
measures.
This paper makes three contributions to the literature by addressing the existing gaps in it. 
Firstly, the study measures ex-ante spoofing, a n u nder-researched t opic i n t he  l iterature by 
providing mechanisms that can be used to identify spoofing in the LOB. Notable studies (Do & 
Putni┼є┼А, 2023; Lee et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2021) discuss the impact of spoofing after it has taken 
place. We believe the ability to detect spoofing beforehand is beneficial for regulators and is 
capable of improving the e iciency of market surveillance systems. Secondly , this study identifies 
the most suitable metrics that can be used to detect potential spoofing in intraday data. Thirdly, 
by employing logistic regression and other statistical methods, we provide an empirical 
perspective to spoofing. The empirical literature on spoofing is  limited owing to  the complex 
nature of the data. We could not obtain transaction-level data due to the implementation of 
post-trade anonymity (PoTA) by Nasdaq Nordic. The exclusion of order identification makes 
it laborious to identify matching orders and differentiate between spoof orders and real orders. 
In instances where market manipulation has not been proven, quantitative analysis can only 
identify potential spoofing. Hence, our results provide circumstantial evidence that can be used 
to formulate anti-spoofing regulations.
The structure of the remaining paper is as follows: Chapter 2 of the paper provides a compre-
hensive review of theoretical literature and empirical studies related to spoofing. Chapter 3, 
outlines the methods used to answer the main research questions. Chapter 4 provides a detailed 
description of the results obtained from empirical analysis. Chapter 5 evaluates to what extent 
the results relate to previous literature and the main hypotheses Finally, Chapter 6 concludes 
the main argument proposed by the paper and recommends avenues of further research.

2 Literature Review
Market Manipulation is not a novel concept as its origins can be traced back to the advent of
financial markets. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) refers to an excerpt by Joseph de la Vega on the Amsterdam
Stock Exchange that compares the financial markets to a theatrical comedy and believes that
market agents are Рђўspeculators who excel in tricksРђЎ. Spoofing is just one spectrum of the РђўtricksРђЎ
manipulators use to distort the market and induce artificial changes in prices. Existing literature
has a wealth of information on trade-based manipulation but a dearth of information on spoofing
and other order-based manipulation strategies. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) and Wang ( 2015) state that most
forms of market manipulation spread asymmetric information in the market which disrupts the
price-setting forces of demand and supply.
2.1 Financial theories in relation to spoofing
The e№гђicient market hypothesis (EMH) posits that stock prices reflect all public and private
informationandtherefore,thefundamentalortruevalueofasecuritycanbeaccuratelypredicted.
Hence, noagentcanbeatthemarket(Malkiel&Fama, 1970). Thus, thereisnoavailablepathway
that manipulators can exploit to realize a profit. Fischel and Ross ( 1991) base their arguments
on the EMH and claim that manipulation is impossible in an e№гђicient market. They believe
that due to perfect competition, no agent has an opportunity to exert market power to influence
prices. Spoofing challenges the EMH by showing that markets can be manipulated, at least in
the short run, through the dissemination of artificial information (fake orders) that ultimately
alters the market price.
Kyle (1985) model of informed trading provides insights into how private information impacts
market prices. The model refers to the trading process as a game between three market agents:
insiders who have valuable private information about the fundamental value of a security, noise
traderswhotradebasedonspeculation, andmarketmakerswhosetthepricebutarenotawareof
whethertheyaretradingwithinsidersornoisetraders. Themarketmakerstrytoadjustpricesby
observingtotalorderflowbytryingtodissectwhichpartoftheorderflowisinformed, originating
from insiders, and which part is uninformed, originating from noise traders. Since insiders have
access to private information they use it to set trade sizes that enable them to maximize profit
without revealing information. In the context of spoofing we believe that spoofers mimic the
behavior of an informed trader by placing large orders they intend to cancel before execution.
Market makers respond to the deceptive order flow by adjusting prices not realizing that the
orders are fake. As a result, spoofers realize a profit due to short-term price fluctuations.
Allen and Gorton ( 1992) show that due to information asymmetry, traders cannot gauge if
manipulative traders have access to private information. The timing of news matters. If good
news in the stock market arrives before the bad news; noise traders can mislead the market by
placing large orders signaling upcoming good news. Additionally, the uncertainties surrounding
themotivationsof large investorswhen they trade instocksintroduce a degree of unpredictability
into the market that can be exploited for profit. When a large investor makes significant trades,
it is challenging for other market participants to discern if the true intent of an investor is based
on genuine beliefs about the securityРђЎs value or if the investor aims to manipulate the market by
artificially inducing price changes. Jarrow ( 1992) identifies that large traders exert market power
5

and
indulge in market manipulation in the securities market. Since large traders are essentially
price-setters, they can easily influence the price in their favor.
Glosten and Milgrom (1985) builds upon the Kyle model by introducing how prices adjust on a
per-trade basis taking informational asymmetry into account. The model outlines how market
makers adjust the bid-ask spread to mitigate losses and reduce adverse selection costs that arise
while trading with informed traders. Based on our opinion, the model can be applied to spoofing
in the following way: spoofers exploit the bid-ask spread by placing large, misleading orders on
one side of the order book. For instance, when spoofers place buy orders, market makers, in
response, raise ask prices to protect against potential losses that could occur by selling at lower
prices to informed traders. This leads to the widening of the bid-ask spread that allows the
spoofer to make more profit, as now they can sell their positions (genuine orders) at a higher
price. Allen and Gorton (1992) devise an equilibrium model based on the Glosten-Milgrom
model, which states that the inclusion of noise traders makes it easier to manipulate the market
as informed traders have an informational advantage.
Chakraborty and Y─▒lmaz (2004) use the findings of the Glosten and Milgrom (1985) and Kyle
(1985) model to showcase that informed traders trading in the long run in multiple periods
manipulate the market by blu№гђing. Informed traders incur losses in the short run; but the
additional noise or misleading news originating from their bluff would enable them to extract
abnormalprofitsinthelongrun. WilliamsandSkrzypacz(2021)adapttheorderbookframework
of the Glosten-Milgrom (1985) model and add another dimension of order cancellation while
trying to study the impact of spoofing on market quality. Firstly, they identify that spoofing
impedes the price discovery process as it obscures trading motives. Secondly, they show that
spoofing increases the bid-ask spread. Thirdly, they demonstrate that spoofing increases the
volatility of returns as posting fake orders and subsequently cancelling them moves prices away
from their fundamental value.
Information asymmetry leads to the breakdown of the notions set forth by the EMH and adds
another layer to the insights provided by the Glosten-Milgrom (1985) and the Kyle (1984) model.
Fishman and Hagerty (1992) devise a single-period equilibrium model of information-based ma-
nipulation. The retail investors in the model realize a profit by posing as informed investors by
disclosing their order quantities. Aggarwal and Wu (2006) state that manipulators can easily
enter the market as most market participants, especially arbitrageurs are always seeking more in-
formation. Brogaard et al. (2018) develop a return variance decomposition model to demonstrate
how different types of information influence stock prices. They find that 37% of the variation
in prices is based on firm-specific public information, 31% of the variation is based on noise
(potential spoofing), 24% of the variation comes from private firm-level information, and only
8% of the variation comes from overall market information.
Wang (2015) argues that the main motive of a spoofer is to defraud investors so information
asymmetry is a necessary condition for spoofing to occur. For informational asymmetry to suc-
cessfully mislead a trader, behavioral finance theories such as herding or animal spirits come
into play. Bikhchandani (2000) suggest that because of herding behavior, the profit-maximizing
investors are interdependent and react similarly to market sentiments. This gives spoofers an
opportunity to inject incorrect information into the market knowing that the majority of the
information-reliant investors would act in the direction that the spoofer intends. In addition to
this Van Bommel (2003) demonstrates an information-based game theory model where a ma-
nipulator deliberately reveals imprecise information to manipulate gullible market participants.
6

The misleading information or РђўrumorРђЎ induces a change in the market price in favor of the
manipulator.
Spoofing has economic, financial, and legal implications. Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) proposes that regulators
are increasingly interested in spoofing as the number of prosecuted cases regarding spoofing has
gone up in recent times. The main problem that legal scholars and regulators face is how to
discern spoofing from normal market behavior. Fischel and Ross ( 1991) posit that it is di№гђicult
to detect manipulation as it is largely based on traderРђЎs intent which is an intangible attribute
that varies among individuals. They believe that it is hard to separate legitimate demand and
supply from fraudulent demand and supply, as all traders constitute the supply and demand
forces of the market. Thel ( 1993) provides a contrarian view by stating that some traders can
have a large bearing on market price as they possess significant market power. The stock market
does not follow the concept of perfect competition propagated by the EMH, and instead, some
large investors can have monopoly power over it, which enables them to control prices.
Pirrong ( 2017) gives a legal perspective to the ongoing debate on market manipulation. He traces
the origin of anti-manipulation laws in the United States and Europe. In the US regulations,
market manipulation has been in place since the early 1920s with the enforcement of the Com-
modity Exchange Act (CEA). The CEA has been fine-tuned to pass the Dodd-Frank Act of 2010,
which explicitly outlawed spoofing. Before the Act, it was di№гђicult to prove manipulation as the
government needed to show Рђюan intent to affect the market price of a commodity and engage
[in] some overt act in furtherance of that intent.РђЮ4To solve this problem, the 2010 Act clearly
defines dubious intent as Рђюa spoofing violation will not occur when the personРђЎs intent when
cancelling a bid or offer before the execution was to cancel such bid or offer as part of a legiti-
mate, good-faith attempt to consummate a tradeРђЮ5. To discern legitimate intent from dubious
intent the government considers Рђюthe market context, the personРђЎs pattern of trading activity
(including fill characteristics), and other relevant facts and circumstancesРђЮ. The 2010 Act has
been developed to mitigate the possibility of another РђЎFlash CrashРђЎ that rocked the US Finan-
cial market in 2010. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) convicted Navinder
Sarao, a high-frequency trader in 2015 for committing fraud by engaging in spoofing and layering
orders in the futures market6. The CFTC elaborated on unique examples of potential spoofing
situations:
1.РђюSubmitting or cancelling bids or offers to overload the quotation system of a registered
entityРђЮ
2.РђюSubmitting or cancelling bids or offers to delay another personРђЎs execution of tradesРђЮ
3.РђюSubmitting or cancelling multiple bids or offers to create an appearance of false market
depthРђЮ
4.РђюSubmitting or cancelling bids or offers with intent to create artificial price movements
upwards or downwardsРђЮ

5See The Law Surrounding Spoofing in the Derivatives and Securities Markets
