Moreover, Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) suggests that regulators have become wary of the growth of high-
frequency trading as it can induce an increase in market manipulation. Canellos et al. ( 2016)
extend the discussion by listing down spoofing cases that happened before and after the Dodd-
Frank Act. Aldridge ( 2013) critiques the effectiveness of anti-spoofing regulations, arguing that
the ambiguity in defining the intent and the rapid evolution of trading technologies pose chal-
lenges to enforcement. Therefore, there is a need for more stringent measures to counter manip-
ulation.
Anti-manipulation laws were developed in Europe with the origin of The Market Abuse Directive
(MAD) ratified in 2003. The directive defines market manipulation, lays down a foundation for
discerning trader intent, and limits fictitious transactions that could distort prices. However, as
Putni┼є┼А ( 2011) outlines, regulators uncovered a sizable РђўLIBOR ScandalРђЎ where major financial
benchmarks such as LIBOR, IBOR, and other foreign exchange benchmarks were being manip-
ulated. As a result, the financial market incurred huge losses as the pricing of many financial
instruments relied on LIBOR. This created a pressing need for the formation of a more com-
prehensive regulation so the Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) was passed in 2016. One notable
aspect of the regulation is the inclusion of potential (ex-ante)market manipulation that gives
regulators greater control on how to define trader intent and prosecute manipulators who intend
to tamper with market forces even if the manipulators fail to cause any real change in the mar-
ket. The Act also prohibits market abuse that could occur due to high-frequency traders (HFTs)
submitting and cancelling orders (spoofing). Miller and Shorter (2016) extensively review the
literature on HFTs and market manipulation and discuss possible regulatory action to counter
spoofing. Fox et al. ( 2022) design a sorting criteria that can help regulators distinguish between
spoofing behavior and normal trading behavior.
2.2 Empirical review and development of hypotheses
Trading in the financial market occurs through a Рђўcontinuous double-actionРђЎ framework called the
limit order book (LOB) (Gould & Bonart, 2016). Trading on most venues occurs between 9:30
AM to 5:00 PM. A queuing system is used in the LOB where active orders rest on top of each
other and are either executed based on price-time priority or cancelled by their owner. During
a typical trading day, a sizeable number of orders are posted in the LOB. Therefore, modeling
order book dynamics is a complex process (Parlour, 1998; Rosu,2009).
Hypothesis 1 :Imbalance in LOB is representative of potential spoofing and is related to sub-
sequent changes in price of a security
Spoofing has been integrated with equilibrium order book dynamics in existing literature. As a
consequence of electronic trading, a modern trader can get a detailed snapshot of the market and
can monitor the demand and supply quantities of their preferred financial instrument (Gould &
Bonart,2016). Due to the popularity of LOB trading, researchers are increasingly interested in
investigating if LOB information can accurately predict future prices.
Taoetal.( 2022)commentonthedi№гђicultythatariseswhendecidingwhichfactorstoincludefrom
thecomplexinformationavailablefromtheLOBtomodelanexperimenttoforecastfutureprices.
They find a solution in previous literature. Numerous studies (Alexander et al., 2013; Bechler &
Ludkovski, 2017; Cartea et al., 2015,2020; Xu et al., 2019; Yang & Zhu, 2015) revolve around
8

the concept of employing order book imbalance which is the difference between the volumes
available for trading at the highest bid and lowest ask prices, i.e., Best Bid and Offer (BBO) as
the most suitable indicator to predict future price movement. One notable feature of spoofing
is that it is a leading contributor to order book imbalance Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023). Moreover,
Withanawasam et al. ( 2018) suggest that some traders manipulate the LOB. They simulate a
LOB based model to demonstrate that large (aggressive) profit more from manipulating liquid
stocks than small (non-aggressive) traders.
Alexander et al. ( 2013) use a stochastic model to study the impact of the top-of-the-order book
imbalance on future price movement. Their model predicts how prices react to trade arrivals.
Gould and Bonart ( 2016) utilize a logistic regression to model the association between order
imbalance and the resulting change in mid-price. Their results show a statistically significant
relationship between the two variables of interest. Xu et al. ( 2019) posit a linear relationship
between imbalance and the subsequent changes in mid-price. They calculate imbalance at multi-
ple stages of the LOB and discover that small tick stocks have weaker goodness of fit than large
tick stocks. Tao et al. ( 2022) use LOB data reported at two levels at Toronto Stock Exchange
Database (TMX) to quantify the influence of weighted imbalance on the movement in prices
to illustrate a model of spoofing behavior. The results indicate that some market-makers use
a spoofing strategy by posting large orders to create imbalance at different levels of LOB to
alter the market depth and induce changes in price. They comment that for a spoofing attempt
to be successful, the genuine market order placed by the spoofer needs to be filled. To discern
spoofing behavior from normal trading behavior they observe the timing of an executed market
order. In the case of legitimate trading the imbalance before order execution occurs at time t-1
and at time t+1 prices adjust to their long-run values. In contrast, under spoofing behavior,
the imbalance occurs at t-1, but the price does not adjust back to its equilibrium value at t+1.
Rather, the market order is executed somewhere in between these two intervals at a different
price, for instance, t+0.5. This change in price is attributed to spoofing. Therefore they suggest
that spoofing occurs within a very short timeframe.
Hypothesis 2: Order Cancel lation in LOB is representative of potential spoofing and is related
to subsequent changes in price of a security.
Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) examine a robust and global sample of prosecuted cases of ex-post ma-
nipulation for a 10-year period ranging from (2010-2020). A typical spoof case consists of a
spoofer placing a small (genuine) order on one side of LOB and subsequently placing a large
(fake) order on the opposite side of the LOB; this induces an imbalance in LOB. This causes a
short-term price movement in favour of the spoofer, the small (genuine) order is then executed at
a favourable price. The large order is then cancelled. They also find that spoofers use a variety
of methods to manipulate the market. Some spoofers trade manually while others trade with the
aid of computer algorithms. Despite the differences in trading approaches, spoofing cases have a
lot of commonalities. All instances of spoofing involve order book imbalance and order cancella-
tion. Therefore, Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) devise intraday and daily metrics to detect spoofing. To
discern which of these metrics are the best proxies for spoofing, they conduct logistic regression
on both daily and intraday data. To improve reliability they use out-of-sample cross-validation
tests and also employ machine learning models to capture the non-linear relationship that exists
between the different metrics. For daily data, the most suitable spoofing proxies are:Рђю Cyclical
Patterns in Market Depth and Cancel lations and T rades on the Opposite Side to Cancels and
QuotesРђЮ. For intraday data, the most suitable spoofing proxies are: Рђю High Quoting Activity,
Unbalanced Quoting Activity (imbalance in market depth), and Abnormal Order Cancel lation
9

RatesРђЮ. Based on their results, they conclude that for intraday data, order imbalance and order 
cancellation are related to changes in mid-price and are good indicators to detect spoofing. Do 
and Putni┼є┼А (2023) state that their metrics are e№гђicient proxies for spoofing and when combined 
with logistic regression, their accuracy in detecting spoofing improves considerably. Hence, their 
metrics provide a solid basis for further research.
Their results support the findings of Williams and Skrzypacz (2021) who develop an equilibrium-
based model inspired by the Glosten-Milgrom model to study the Canadian market. Their 
model encapsulates the impact of spoofing on market quality by including the aspect of order 
cancellation within the framework of the Glosten-Milgrom model. They discover that sizable 
cancellations in LOB induce changes in the equilibrium price of a financial instrument.
2.3 Price discovery and spoofing
Putni┼є┼А (2011) implies that manipulation leads to the creation of Рђўartificial pricesРђЎ prices that do 
not affect real or true equilibrium value. Rather, they represent false or speculative value. Hence, 
spoofing u ndermines m arket e №гђiciency be cause pr ices te mporarily re flect art ificial conditions 
rather than true market fundamentals. Moreover, spoofing can have dire implications, such as 
loss of trust in the market, and can lead to temporary market failure akin to the 2010  Flash 
Crash.
Wang (2015) delves into spoofing occurring in the T aiwanese index futures markets. They show 
that both retail and institutional investors indulge in spoofing and that it occurs on both sides of 
the LOB. Spoofers generate a profit when market volatility and volumes are high as it is easier 
for the spoofer to hide their orders, and in the case of a spoof-sell strategy, the resulting impact 
on price, volume, and spread is negative. Subsequentl y, he discusses that a spoof-buy strategy 
influences price, volume and volatility p ositivel y. Furthermore, they demonstrate that the fate 
of a successful spoof strategy depends on pre-existing market conditions. Spoofing will gain 
more positive returns for the spoofer if the market is highly volatile and traders are uncertain 
about price changes. An increase in price due to spoofing will give rise to inventory risk and 
also increase the adverse selection cost as other traders might have more credible information to 
mitigate this market makers will widen the bid-ask spread. Therefore, spoofing can disrupt the 
market. Extending this argument Williams and Skrzypacz (2021) show that spoofing disrupts 
the price-setting mechanism, widens the bid-ask spread, and raises the occurrence of rapidly 
fluctuating returns (volatility).
Wang et al. (2021) provide an agent-based model description of fluctuations in prices caused by 
spoofing in financial markets. In a single security model, agents interact through LOB based on 
their degree of information. Agents follow either of the two approaches: РђўHeursitic belief 
learningРђЎ (spoofable) as this trader is information-reliant and diligently follows the LOB or РђўZero 
intelligenceРђЎ as per which the trader does not rely on information from LOB. The spoofable 
traders make the market more prone to manipulation, and spoofing tampers with market qual-
ity by reducing total surplus, injecting false information, and impeding price discovery. They 
recommend that regulations need  to be designed to discourage spoofing.
10

Brogaard
etal.(2022)deviseanexperimenttotestopposingtheoriesontheimpactofspoofingon
marketquality. TheystudytheCanadianEquityMarketusingtheIIROCdatasetwhichincludes
trader ID, quote data, and trade data. Firstly, they find 25 unsuccessful spoofing orders based
on median stock day observations. Secondly, they investigate spoofing diversity by using SEC
litigation releases and lagged spoofing profitability to gauge the impact of spoofing on market
quality. They find that spoofing induces an increase in trading costs, a rise in return volatility,
and a decrease in price discovery. Therefore, spoofing impairs price discovery and liquidity. Their
results are in line with theories proposed by Williams and Skrzypacz (2021). Additionally, they
argue that spoofing is hard to detect therefore, they use a six-step categorization strategy that
scans trade and quote data to determine spoofing. Furthermore, spoofing is more common in
trading days with intermediate transaction costs, return volatility and high price e№гђiciency.
Some researchers have opposing views regarding the impact of market manipulation on market
quality. Cherian and Jarrow (1995) find that a proportional response in prices makes manip-
ulation unprofitable. On the contrary, some studies show that manipulation improves market
quality. Hanson and Oprea (2009) build an empirical model where the manipulative market
maker is a noise trader and show that the potential for abusing the market improves information
collection as informed investors generate a higher profit, which increases price e№гђiciency.
2.4 Overview of other empirical studies on spoofing
Lee et al. ( 2009) analyze spoofing by compiling a dataset that contains intraday order data and
trade data and order identification data taken from the Korea Exchange (KRX). Till the end
of 2001, KRX had a unique order disclosure rule designed to aid investors with more accurate
information about the demand and supply of a financial instrument. However, this informational
transparency encourages spoofing and Рђўmicrostructure-basedРђЎ manipulation. In January 2002,
KRX introduced opacity, and total order quantities were hidden on both sides of the order
book. The data ranging from November 2001 to February 2002 captures spoofing that occurs
from November to December 2001 and showcases the effects of microstructure changes due to
regulatory change from January 2002 onwards. The results exemplify that prior to the rule
change spoofing was more rampant. Consequently, more informational transparency is positively
associated with more spoofing.
Cartea et al. (2019) conduct an empirical analysis of spoofing by devising a model where a trader
spoofs the LOB. The trader sells both limit orders (LOs), which are orders sold or bought at
a pre-determined price, and market orders (MOs), which are orders sold at the best current
market price, and simultaneously buys a considerable number of LO (spoof orders) to tamper
with order book volume. They predict that the payoff from a spoof strategy is higher than the
payoff from a non-spoof strategy. Spoofing is advantageous due to roundtrip trades, less usage
of market orders, and the possibility of selling at a higher price if LOB is biased toward the
buy side. Additionally, they shed light on the legal implications of spoofing by discussing that
regulatory action, such as fines, can deter spoofers.
11

2.5 Spoofing window
A typical spoof window consists of three phases. Phase I begins with the placement of large
fake orders on one side of the LOB to create a false perception of demand and supply pressure
to induce changes in the price. Phase II occurs after the price change in which the fake orders
are cancelled. The window culminates with phase III when the genuine orders are executed at a
favorable price.
Based on the framework developed by Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) we study the first two phases of the
spoofing window. We are unable to capture the price impact of phase III due to the lack of order
IDs. Due to recent regulatory changes such as MiFID II and PoTA (Post-trade anonymity).
Forinstance in Figure 1, phase I begins withthe spoofer submitting a РђўfalseРђЎ bid (ask) order inthe
LOB to drive the mid-price in the desired direction by the manipulator. Mid-price is defined as
theaverageofthebestbidandthebestaskpricesintheorderbook(115.83). Whenthemid-price
achieves the increased (decreased) level (115.85) intended by the spoofer, phase II of the spoofing
window begins where they cancel the alleged РђЎfalseРђЎ bid (ask) order (- in Figure 1) and execute
their РђўgenuineРђЎ ask (bid) order on the opposite side of the LOB at their favorable relatively lower
(higher) mid-price (115.82), initiating phase III of the spoofing window for successful execution
of their spoofing attempt.
12

3 Empirical Setting and Research Design
3.1 Empirical setting
We investigate the presence of spoofing in the Swedish stock market. For this purpose, we
analyze OMXS30 index futures contracts listed on Nasdaq Stockholm to identify the market
manipulation practice. OMXS30 is the leading capitalisation-weighted stock market index of the
Stockholm Stock Exchange and comprises the 30 largest and most actively traded stocks on the
exchange. Being a derivative of the OMXS30 index, OMXS30 futures contracts allow traders to
speculate on the future price of the underlying index. The OMXS30 futures feature a contract
size of SEK 100 and are available with 3 maturities РђЊ 3, 18, and 60 months.
We select the OMXS30 futures for this study mainly due to three reasons. Firstly, the underlying
index of the derivative security features high liquidity, and thus, futures contracts guarantee a
su№гђicient volume of data for the analysis. Secondly, sole concentration on Nasdaq Stockholm, a
single-market exchange, allows us to isolate potential spoofing activities within a clearly defined
regulatory framework. Thirdly, Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023) note in their comprehensive observed
sample of 28 cases from the US and UK that the futures market has been the most attractive
venue for spoofing. For instance, one of the most prolific spoofing cases, the РђЎFlash CrashРђЎ CFTC
vs Sarao, involved manipulation in the E-mini futures market. Furthermore, Kyle ( 1983) seminal
study of manipulation on the futures market identifies that the e№гђiciency of the futures market
is heavily reliant on accurate information. The informed trader or the potential spoofer in our
case can indulge in market abuse by profiting over the lack of information possessed by other
traders Kyle ( 1983). The popularity of high-frequency trading and the dynamic nature of the
futures market provide accessible venues for spoofers to manipulate the market (Brogaard et al.,
2022). Hence, the approach focused on the futures market strengthens our ability to identify
and analyze spoofing behavior effectively.
3.2 Data and data sources
We collect the LOB data for OMXS30 futures from the Tick History database maintained by the
London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG). We have chosen the 3-month contract for our sample
period, starting from May 2019 until July 2019. We chose this specific timeframe as this period
precedes the COVID-19 pandemic and helps us ensure that our analysis is not clouded by the
substantial market disruptions caused by the global health crisis. Moreover, the observed sample
is large enough to filter out noise and mimics the cycle of a three-month futures contract.
LOB data offers a granular view of the order activity and a detailed reflection of market depth
by providing bid and ask prices for six price levels, with level 1 representing the best available
bid and ask. The LOB data allows us to analyze when orders are placed and when they are
cancelled (Mendon├Дa & De Genaro, 2020). This is specifically true for large orders that might
be used by manipulators to create a misleading impression of market depth.
14

The LOB data enables us to identify potential spoofing instances, if any, by examining the time
difference between order placement and order cancellation and the impact on the price discovery
of the security due to aggressive bid and ask placements and cancellations.
3.3 Key variables and metrics
We calculate a total of seven variables using the LOB data for the OMXS30 futures contract:
1. AskSize t: It refers to the total order quantity at the top six asklevels for the security at
time t.
2. BidSize t: It records the total order quantity at the top six bidlevels for the security at
time t.
3. EntryAskSize t: This variable captures the increase in the aggregate volume of the orders
resting on the top six asklevels for the security at time t. It is equal to 0 if there is no
increase in the aggregate volume.
4. EntryBidSize t: This variable measures the rise in the total quantity of orders lying on
the top six bidlevels at time t. In the event that the aggregate volume does not rise, it
equals 0.
5. CancelAskSize t: It is the decrease in the aggregate volume of the orders resting on the
top six asklevels of the security at time t(equal to 0 if there is no decrease in the total
volume).
6. CancelBidSize t: It is the reduction in the aggregate volume of the orders resting on the
top six bidlevels of the security at time t(equal to 0 if there is no decrease in the total
volume).
7. MidPriceChange (MPC) : It is defined as the change in the average of the bid and ask
prices at L1. We calculate MPC for two time intervals, namely t +1and t+2, separately.
We construct three metrics, namely High Quoting Activity, Unbalanced Quoting Activity, and
Abnormal cancellations, using the above-mentioned first six variables. The construction of the
metrics is inspired by the work done by Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023), to capture the market depth.
However, we modify them to capture the side of the LOB used for possible spoofing, to answer
our first research question aimed at identifying the characteristics of a spoofing window. All
the metrics are computed on one-second intervals since market depth is a dynamic concept and
with the increase in high-frequency trading, multiple and simultaneous changes can occur in the
LOB (Debie et al., 2022). Thus, one-second intervals allow us to track market depth changes
in a precise manner with the possibility of detecting potential spoofing attempts that might get
missed with lower frequencies (Battalio et al., 2013; Brogaard et al., 2018; Colliard & Hoffmann,
2017). The time interval of one second also allows us to capture the time difference between the
simultaneous fake order cancellations and subsequent order placement on the opposite side of
the LOB during the potential spoofing attempts in the LOB. We use mid-price for our analysis
as it provides a better representation of the price prevailing in the LOB. Firstly, mid-price is
calculated as the average of best bid and ask prices so it reduces the impact of outliers. Secondly,
15

mid-price reflects fair market value rather than a value based on nominal factors. Thirdly, mid-
price smoothens out the volatility in data series that might arise if closing or opening prices are
used instead. Hence, the usage of mid-price increases the e№гђiciency of statistical analysis.
Metric 1: High Quoting Activity (HQA)
Spoofing techniques rely on deception. Intending to cancel the orders prior to their execution,
spoofers usually place manipulative limit orders on one side of the order book, either bid or ask.
Even though these orders might not be honored later, their existence in the LOB drastically
changes the perception of market depth.
Depending on whether a significant number of spoof orders are placed on the ask or bid side,
they may give the false appearance that there is an overwhelming supply or demand for the
security in the market. We capture this resting of limit orders in the LOB through our HQA
metric, which is calculated in the following manner -
HQA t=max tРѕѕs(
EntryAskSize tРѕњEntryBidSize t
AskSize t+BidSize t)
Time trefers to phase I of the spoofing window when the alleged fake order is placed in the LOB.
Metric 2: Unbalanced Quoting (UQ)
A spoofing strategyРђЎs resting (unexecuted) limit orders at any one moment are probably going
to be extremely imbalanced (more bid volume than ask volume, or vice versa). This deceives
other market participants by giving a false indication of oneРђЎs buying or selling interest and thus
impacting the prices of the security. We capture this dimension of a spoofing attempt through
our UQ metric:
UQ t=max tРѕѕs(
AskSize tРѕњBidSize t
AskSize t+BidSize t)
Metric 3: Abnormal Cancellation
High order cancellations are a crucial indicator of a potential spoofing attempt. This is due to
the fact that stacked orders in the LOB are usually cancelled because they are not meant to
be executed and are initially submitted only to create a false impression of market depth. We
capture order cancellations in the LOB using our AC metric:
ACt+1=max t+1Рѕѕs(CancelAskSize t+1РѕњCancelBidSize t+1
AskSize t+1+BidSize t+1)
Time t+1refers to phase II of the spoofing window when the alleged fake order is cancelled.
16

3.4 Research design
We construct a contingency table to investigate the characteristics of a typical spoofing window
and to identify the most suitable metrics for identifying the manipulative practice in OMXS30
futures contracts trading. For studying the characteristics, we analyze how many times the
cancellation of a potentially fake order is followed by the placement of a so-called genuine order
on the other side of the order book. For this, we examine the instances when a cancelled ask
(bid) order, captured by the variable CancelAskSize t(CancelBidSize t) is followed by a negative
(positive) value of the UQ t+1metric. We choose the UQ metric for this analysis as it indicates
which side of the order book is relatively more imbalanced. This examination provides us with
two aspects to answer our first research question. Firstly, the time difference between such
cancelled and subsequent genuine orders on average. And secondly, if there exists a preferred
side of the LOB which is preferred by the spoofers.
We do not have thresholds to quantify what length of the time difference is considered short, and
thus, we make an assumption that a spoof trader manipulates the LOB within one second on
average. Our assumption is based on Tao et al. (2022), who states that spoofing occurs in a very
short time frame, and on Debie et al. (2022), who show traditional spoofing visualizations for
one-second snapshots of LOB. We choose the median for representing the average time difference
as it is a better representative of the data here because it is relatively less sensitive than the
mean to the outliers, which may be present in the data set due to genuine order book activity. To
identify the preferred side of the LOB used for the alleged manipulation, we study the instances
of order cancellations and subsequent placement on the opposite sides of the LOB.
To answer our second research question relating to the identification of the most suitable metrics
for identifying potential spoofing, we split the metric values into three categories namely greater
than 0, equal to 0, and less than 0. While the categorical values at greater than and less than 0
help us identify the directional relationship between the metrics and MPC, the categorical value
at equal to 0 supports the comprehensiveness of the relationships. We study these categorical
values of the metrics at time twith the return of the OMXS30 futures contracts in time t+1. We
calculate the return of the security as the difference in its LOB mid-price captured by variable
MPC at time t,t+1, and t+2, which is representative of phase III of the spoofing window when
the so-called genuine order is placed on the other side of the LOB.
The expectation of the direction of return depends on the side of the LOB on which the spoofer
attempts to do the manipulation and the phase of spoofing in which they are operating. We
divide our metrics HQA tand UQ tas phase I metrics or entry metrics and AC t+1as the phase
II metric or exit metric. If the spoofer wants to placetheir genuine orders on the ask (bid) side,
they will begin with placing their dubious orders on the bid (ask) side in time t, with the aim
to impose buying (sel ling) pressure in the order book and drive the MPC up (down) in t+1.
When the mid-price of the security has moved up (down) enough to match the desired level of
the spoofer, they will canceltheir dubious order in time t+1on the bid (ask) side of the LOB,
putting sel ling (buying) pressure in the LOB and benefit from the low (high) mid-price. Upon
successful execution of their real order on the ask (bid) side, the mid-price is expected to go down
(up) in time t+2.
We list our behavioral expectations of change in MPC at time t+1and t+2following different
values of HQA and UQ at time tand AC at t+1in all possible scenarios in Table 1:

Moreover, to distinguish potential spoofing from normal trading activity in the LOB, we study
the results of the contingency table parallel to the time difference between the cancellation of the
fake order in phase II of the spoofing window and the subsequent placement of the genuine order
on the opposite side of the LOB in phase III. Since the time difference aspect is captured by our
first research question, the validity of the result for our second research question is dependent
on the result of the first research question.
3.5 Logistic regression
To answer our third research question, we study the impact of potential spoofing on the price
discoveryoftheOMXS30futurescontractsintheSwedishmarketfurtherusinglogisticregression
with the help of the indicators and the variable MPC. We choose logistic regression for our study
as it is a well-suited statistical technique for analyzing the relationship between independent
variables and a binary dependent variable. We run the regression separately for the first two
phases of the potential spoofing window. In both cases, the binary dependent variable is Y which
captures the the probability of an upward mid-price change in the LOB after a possible spoofing
activity. The probability Pis the probability of success which refers to the likelihood of an
upward shift in mid-price. In contrast, (1-P)refers to the probability of failure, and thus, in our
case, it captures the outcomes where the upward shift in mid-price does not occur. Thus, we code
MPC as a binary variable Y to take value 1 if the mid-price increases from the previous second
and 0 if the change in the mid-price remains unchanged or experiences a downward movement
in the succeeding second. Thus, the probability of Y = 1 is taken on the left-hand side of the
regression equation.
In total, we run five logistic regressions. Model 1-4 study the impact of phase I metrics HQA t,
UQ t, and an interaction term in the form of a dummy variable Dtas the independent variables
on the dependent variable Y in time t+1i.e., phase II of the spoofing window. Whereas, in
model 5, we only have the metric AC t+1as the independent variable representative of phase II
to study its impact on Y in time t+2i.e., phase III of the spoofing window.

3.6 Research ethics
The primary ethical research concern in this study is data management in relation to the terms
and conditions established by LSEG. We consent that the information is solely used for schol-
arly or informative purposes and that any copyrighted content is cited appropriately, wherever
required. We state that there have been no conflicts of interest that would affect how the study
is conducted, how the data is analyzed, or how the findings are interpreted. Moreover, for this
study, we have received research guidance from the market surveillance and systems design Fin-
tech Scila AB, however, the association has been completely research motivated, and no influence
has been shadowed by this association on our findings and analysis.

4 Findings & Analysis
4.1 Descriptive statistics
To answer our first research question What are the characteristics of a spoofing window? , we
study two dimensions of a spoofing window - firstly, the time difference observed between the
orders that have been potentially РђўflippedРђЎ by the spoofers and secondly, the side of the LOB
that has been preferred by the spoofers relatively more.
Table2presents the summary statistics on both these aspects. The median of the time difference
observed between cancellations of the РђўfakeРђЎ orders on the ask side and the subsequent placement
oftheРђўgenuineРђЎordersonthebidsidestandsatonesecond. Theminimumandmedianofthetime
difference between order cancellations on the bid side and the following РђўrealРђЎ orders on the ask
side is also one second. The short time span of one second on average between cancellation and
subsequent placement of orders on the opposite sides of the order book matches our expectation,
which suggests that the data is indicative of spoofing. In normal scenarios where spoofing is not
present, there could be order cancellations and subsequent order flows, however, the probability
for the time span between the two, as short as one second on average, is low.

Which are the relevant metrics for detecting potential spoofing in the Swedish Market?
In phase I of the potential spoofing window, as expected, the positive values of HQA tmatch
with an overall negative price impact, given we have more negative values for MPC t+1than

Thus, we obtain a negative relationship between HQA tand MPC t+1. We observe a similar and
anticipated a№гђinity between UQ tand MPC t+1. When UQ tis positive, 9.52% more negative

times the mid-price moves upward relatively more than the downward swing. It is interesting to
note that the negative relationship observed between UQ tand MPC t+1is stronger than the one
discovered between HQA tand MPC t+1.
In phase II of the spoofing window, we analyze if metric AC is a relevant metric for spotting

This is again in line with our expected relationship between the metric and the price impact
caused by it in the subsequent time interval. The number of positive price changes at t+2 is

4.2 Logistic regression
The descriptive statistics mentioned in Section( 4.1) jointly indicate the presence of potential
spoofing in the Swedish stock market during our sample period. To answer our third and final
researchquestion What is the impact of spoofing on price discovery? , werun fivemodelsoflogistic
regressions as explained in section 3.5.
We divide the regression models as per the two phases of the possible spoofing window to analyze
the impact on the mid-price of the security separately due to the placement of the alleged fake
orders and their subsequent cancellation.
The results of all five logistic models are present in Appendix 1. For Model 1, which studies the
relationship between HQA tand Y t+1, we obtain a positive coe№гђicient of 0.0652 for HQA twith
a p-value of 0.006 which is below the typical significance level of 0.05 and is thus significant.
However, the resulting relationship between HQA tand Y t+1isnotin line with our expectation
which is that of a negative relationship between the the dependent and independent variables.
Also, the intercept term for model 1 stands at -0.0044, indicating negative log odds of Y t+1even
in the absence of HQA t.
We explore the relationship between HQA tand Y t+1further with marginal effects. The calcu-
lated marginal effect for HQA ton Y t+1is again positive at 0.0163, meaning that when HQA t
changes by 1 unit, the log odds of Y increase by 0.0163 units. This is contrary to our expectation
of a negative relationship between HQA tand MPC t+1. The result is highly precise with a low
standard error of 0.006 and statistically significant with a large absolute z-score of 2.763 along
with a p-value of 0.006.
Model2exhibitsanegativerelationshipbetweenUQ tandMPC t+1, inlinewithourexpectations.
The statistical significance of this indirect relationship is confirmed by a low p-value which is
lower than 0.05. The result is also highly precise with a low standard error of 0.002. Also,
since the coe№гђicient of the constant term is positive, the model substantiates that the positive
log-odds of Y t+1exist due to negative values of UQ t. However, we observe that the coe№гђicient
of the constant term is not statistically significant as its p-value is 0.811.
The calculated marginal effect for Model 2 again shows that the log odds of Y t+1decrease by
approximately 0.0029 with a one-unit increase in UQ t. Here, the negative relationship between
MPC tand UQ tis preserved as well. Moreover, the absolute value of the z-score of 5.677 coupled
with a p-value of 0.000, which make us conclude that the effect is statistically significant.

Model 3 checks the relationship between Y t+1and both HQA tand UQ ttogether, with the help

Moreover, a negative coe№гђicient of the intercept term in the model signals that the log odds of
Y are not positive in the absence of D, further strengthening the joint importance of HQA t
and UQ tin analyzing the impact on the price of OMXS30 futures contracts in the presence of
potential spoofing. The regression result for D is significant with a large absolute value of z-score
at 5.137 and a p-value of 0.05.
Thus, Model 3 and Model 4 together further confirm our results in Section 4.1, wherein we
ascertain that HQA tis a relevant metric but a weak one and add to it that it can help detect

In Model 4, we perform the most comprehensive study for phase I of the so-called spoofing

not as expected, for UQ tand D tthey are in line with our expectation. The results are preserved
when calculating the marginal effects of all explanatory variables on Y t+1. Also, all the results
for Model 4 are statistically significant, with a p-value of 0.05 and large absolute z-scores except
the intercept term of the model, which suggests the likelihood of a price decrease in the absence
of all the independent variables.
In summary, UQ timpacts the mid-price of OMXS30 futures contract negatively in the following
second, and HQA tmaintains a positive relationship with it. Also, our interaction term con-
structed by combining HQA tand UQ tfeatures a negative impact on the subsequent mid-price
observed at a difference of one second.
Model 5, which studies the relationship between the phase II metric AC t+1and Y t+2, helps

The model shows that there exists a positive relationship between AC t+1and Y t+2, which

4.3 Robustness tests
We ensure the robustness of our methodological approach in multiple ways. Firstly, we adopt
a hybrid methodological approach, integrating both contingency table and logistic regression
analysis. This enables us to capture the intricacies of the relationship between our metrics and
the mid-price change in the LOB from different perspectives, ensuring a robust understanding
of the underlying dynamics.
Secondly, under the logistic regression, we use a total of 5 models to check the relative impact of
our dependent variables in isolation as well as in combination on the probability of an upward
swing in the mid-price. We also use an interaction term to gauge the combined effect of two
main predictor variables of unbalanced quoting activity in the LOB under Model 3 and Model
4 of our regression analysis. Furthermore, to check the presence of multicollinearity in Model 4,
25

we calculate the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) values for the variables HQ t, UQ t, and D tand
observe them all to be low, ranging from approximately 1.142 to 1.217. Typically, a VIF value
below10indicatesthatmulticollinearityisnotaconcern. Inlogisticregression,heteroskedasticity
can lead to biased standard errors, affecting the reliability of coe№гђicient estimates and hypothesis
tests. Thus, to address this issue, we use the covariance matrix type HC1 which adjusts the
standard errors to correct for heteroskedasticity by ensuring that the standard errors of the
coe№гђicient estimates remain reliable, even when there are varying levels of error variance across
observations.
Furthermore, we extend our analysis beyond mere estimation by subjecting our contingency table
to a chi-square statistical test and our logistic regression models to a likelihood-ratio (LLR) test.
4.3.1 Chi-square statistical test
We run a chi-square statistical test of independence on our contingency table to determine
whether there is a significant association between our categorical values of HQA t, and UQ twith
MPC t+1and AC t+1with MPC t+2. For HQA tand MPC t+1, the chi2test statistic stands at
630.97 with an extremely low p-value of 3.06├Ќ10-135. These findings underscore the robustness of
theassociationbetweenthevariables, emphasizingthesignificanceoftheirrelationshipwithinthe
dataset. ForUQ tandMPC t+1, weobserveachi2teststatisticof78.8407whichindicatesahighly
significant association between the two categorical variables. The p-value associated with the chi-
square statistic is 3.07├Ќ10-16, which is exceedingly small. This indicates that the probability of
observing such an extreme result (or more extreme) under no association between the variables is
extremely low. For AC t+1and MPC t+2also, the chi-square test yields a significant result with №┐┐2
equal to 464.91 and a p-value less than 0.001. Based on the robustness test for our contingency
table, we confidently conclude that there is a statistically significant association between the
categorical values of the metrics and the subsequent mid-price movement, and thus, all three
metrics are suitable for detecting potential spoofing in the Swedish stock market. Thus, the
contingency table and the chi-square test yield the same results.
26

4.3.2 Likelihood-ratio test
We run a likelihood-ratio (LLR) test for our logistic regression models with the null hypothesis
that the models have no explanatory power. LLR test assesses the overall goodness of fit of the
model by comparing the log-likelihood of the model to a null model with no predictors. Table
5illustrates the LLR p-values for all the five logistic regression models. For all of the models
except Model 5, we observe a very low LLR p-value indicating that all the models significantly
improve the fit over the null model. Therefore, we have evidence to reject the null hypothesis.
However, for Model 5, the LLR p-value is 0.1281, which indicates that the improvement in fit
compared to the null model is not statistically significant at a conventional significance level of
0.05. This suggests that the inclusion of the independent variable AC t+1does not significantly
enhance the explanatory power of the model for predicting Y t+2. Thus, the results for all our
logistic regression models are consistent with the LLR test.

5 Discussion
5.1 Discussion of findings
The findings of this thesis suggest order book imbalance illustrated by Unbalanced Quoting
(UQ) is the most suitable metric to identify potential spoofing, whilst High Quoting Activity
(HQA) exhibits to a a weaker and AC to be the weakest proxies for identifying spoofing behavior
in the Swedish market. Moreover, the negative yet significant relationship that UQ exhibits
with change in mid-price (MPC) is in line with the first hypothesis whilst HQA represents a
positive relationship with MPC, against the first hypothesis. Additionally, order cancellations
demonstrated by Abnormal Cancellations (AC) yield insignificant results in the logistic model.
According to the findings from the contingency table, all three metrics are suitable indicators
of potential spoofing. Hence, based on our analysis of both statistical approaches, order book
imbalance is a superior ex-ante spoofing proxy compared to order cancellations. Therefore, a
change in mid-price prevailing for OMXS30 futures is impacted by an unbalanced order flow in
the LOB.
The short-term price movements could either be attributed to some form of spoofing or be an
embodiment of normal trading behavior. Since the observed data outlines ex-ante spoofing, it is
hard to discern whether the obtained results are solely based on spoofing behavior. However, we
make this distinction by noting that spoofing usually occurs in a very short time span equivalent
to one second. The results support the findings of Gould and Bonart ( 2016) who also fit a
logistic regression between Рђўqueue imbalanceРђЎ and Рђўdirection of subsequent mid-price movementРђЎ
and demonstrate a statistically significant relationship. Other researchers, such as (Alexander
et al.,2013; Bechler & Ludkovski, 2017; Cartea et al., 2020; Cont et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2019;
Yang & Zhu, 2015), also recommend that imbalance in the LOB is a simplistic yet powerful
determinant to forecast future prices. These findings are crucial as it is di№гђicult to separate
normal trading behavior from spoofing behavior.
Previous literature suggests that imbalance reflects buying or selling pressure in LOB depending
on which side of LOB a greater number of orders are entered. One way to identify spoofing
behavior is to look at the time difference between order cancellation and subsequent order place-
ment on the other side of the LOB. Another way is to use order IDs to check if matching orders
are placed by the same trader. If a trader uses considerably large orders and keeps on cancelling
them and subsequently placing orders on the opposite side of the LOB, the trader might be
involved in spoofing. To substantiate our findings we use well-established spoofing proxies in-
spired by Do and Putni┼є┼А ( 2023). Moreover, we take inspiration from Tao et al. ( 2022) and divide
order imbalance (phase 1) and order cancellation (phase 2) based on time (one second). Some
researchers assume that a spoof trader would use manual or computer programs to manipulate
the LOB within one second. Our results support the arguments posed by Tao et al. ( 2022) and
Debie et al. ( 2022) who suggest that spoofing can occur within a very short time horizon such
as one second.
An indirect yet significant relationship between order book imbalance and the likelihood of an
upward mid-price revision implies that spoofing more orders on ask side of the LOB for instance
canincreaseinquantitysupplied(moreaskorders), alteringtheperceptionofmarketparticipants
and causing a downward shift in the mid-price. As a result, spoofers can then execute their buy
28

orders at a cheaper, more favorable price. Regardless of which side of the LOB a spoofer chooses
to use for the manipulation, the imbalance in the market caused by unmatched orders disrupts
the order flow and leads to changes in the mid-price.
Based on our results from statistical analysis, order cancellation is weakly indicative of potential
spoofing. Also, AC exhibits a statistically insignificant relationship with the probability of an
upward movement in MPC under our logistic regression model. Our result for AC can be
validated to an extent with the observations made by Khomyn and Putni┼є┼А ( 2021) who state
that the impact of order cancellation is contingent on trader type. A high-frequency trader
usually has a high order cancellation rate that can represent normal trading behavior as opposed
to spoofing behavior. Therefore, it is hard to determine whether cancellations in the LOB in the
observed sample are indicative of spoofing. Therefore, our quantitative model outlines instances
of ex-ante spoofing.
Nevertheless, thesuitabilityofACasanex-antespoofingmetricissubstantiatedbytheresultswe
derive from the contingency table. Moreover, the positive direction of the relationship (although
insignificant) between order cancellation and the likelihood of an upward mid-price revision
observed in our sample aligns with previous literature. Williams and Skrzypacz ( 2021) study the
Canadian market and employ an equilibrium-based model showcasing that more cancellations
increase the likelihood of short-term price movements. Additionally, Wang ( 2015) suggests that
under a buy-spoof strategy, placement of large fake orders on the buy side and their subsequent
cancellation increase the likelihood of an upward mid-price revision, which thus, allows the
spoofer to execute genuine orders on the sell side. It is important to note that the observed
sample does not comprise transaction-level data such as order IDs which enables gauging if the
same trader placed matching orders on opposite sides of the LOB. As a result, the overarching
impact of order cancellation in our analysis is under-represented.
Our findings concerning the more attractive side of the LOB for manipulation outline that
a spoofer is 26.62% points more likely to indulge in buy-side spoofing compared to sell-side
spoofing. Our study not only contributes to the scant literature concerning this domain, but
also extends the argument posed by Cartea et al. ( 2020) that a spoof-buy strategy has a higher
payoff than a spoof-sell strategy if the LOB is more inclined towards the buy side.
Moreover, based on an analysis of prosecuted spoofing cases in Sweden, we can reasonably
conclude that spoofing does occur in the Swedish market. This could be largely due to the fact
that a rise in automation and the ease of high-frequency trading can encourage more spoofing.
For instance, in one of the cases, a trader was able to spoof the Swedish market from a beach in
Italy. A spoofer can now easily monitor the forces of demand and supply that exist in the LOB
of their preferred financial instrument and use this information to their advantage. Furthermore,
HFTs increase the profit potential of a spoof strategy (Putni┼є┼А, 2011). Algorithmic trading
systems can allow a spoofer to replicate its manipulative strategy across trading venues, allowing
them to maximize their profit.
29

Results
for both empirical approaches suggest that instances of potential spoofing adversely
affect market quality as prices change based on the composition of order flow. Brogaard et al.
(2022) argue that spoofing causes short-term price movements that are favorable for a spoofer
but detrimental to overall market quality. The noise that spoofers introduce by posting more
orders on one side tampers with informational e№гђiciency and increases adverse selection costs.
Theoretically, EMHsuggeststhatpricesreflectallpublicandprivateinformation. Spoofersinject
misleading information that is incorporated into transitory prices signalling a market anomaly
that challenges the notions proposed by the EMH. Brogaard et al. (2018) demonstrate that 31%
of the variation in prices is explained by the variation in noise. This implies that a spoofer will
mostly be successful in abusing the market as the effect of noise is incorporated in mid-price. A
speculativeinvestortradingbasedonmisleadinginformation(noise)willexhibitherdingbehavior
and adjust their order flow, leading to a change in the mid-price. Therefore, spoofing depresses
market quality.
One of the most serious economic implications of spoofing is its interference with Adam SmithРђЎs
РђЎinvisible hand РђЎ. Spoofing disrupts the normal price-setting forces of demand and supply and
creates welfare losses for society. Kyle (1985) suggests that a financial market is composed of in-
formed traders and uninformed (noise traders). They refer to informed traders as РђЎmanipulatorsРђЎ
as they use informational advantage at the expense of uninformed traders to generate a profit.
Therefore, spoofing benefits informed traders and encourage rent-seeking behavior. On the con-
trary, an uninformed trader suffers as their trading strategy is based on deceptive information.
Additionally, spoofing has serious legal implications. Regulatory authorities have to constantly
update regulations to match the pace of changing market trends. Regulators are faced with the
dilemma of discerning the true intent of a market trader. Therefore, there is a pressing need to
design an e№гђicient market surveillance system that detects spoofing.
The creation of an Рђўartificial priceРђЎ as outlined by changes in mid-price in the short run, indicated
by the results of this paper has serious implications. Firstly, it gives a misleading impression of
market depth. Secondly, it reduces informational e№гђiciency severely impacting uninformed retail
investors who trade based on the prevailing market sentiment. Thirdly, as prices do not reflect
fundamental values the overall element of trust in the market is reduced. Lastly, disrupting
the price-setting mechanism of demand and supply makes the market more prone to market
failure similar to the 2010 Flash crash. Spoofing inhibits the price-setting mechanism and skews
the incentives of market participants. Any upheaval in the equilibrating forces of demand and
supply will decrease the e№гђiciency of the allocation of resources. Furthermore, spoofing can pose
significant financial losses. For instance, the J.P. Morgan spoofing case resulted in a loss of $300
million for the US economy. Additionally, the LIBOR scandal in UK also had hefty implications
as some financial instruments relied on LIBOR to set prices. Putni┼є┼А (2011) discusses that short-
term price reversals have a direct bearing on market quality as they increase market volatility.
By creating an illusion of an increased demand or supply, spoofers can drive prices up or down,
making the market more volatile. This volatility can affect not only individual securities but
also market indices and the broader financial system.
5.2 Limitations
Existingliteraturerepresentstheex-posteffectsofmanipulationanditsimpactonpricediscovery.
Putni┼є┼А (2011) points out that the ex-ante effects of spoofing, such as the effect of prices and
30

liquidity have not piqued the interest of previous researchers. Therefore, it is difficicult to 
comment on the efficacy of the results of this paper as it adopts a novel approach to  detecting ex-
ante spoofing. Furthermore, the paucity of empirical research further complicates selecting a 
suitable sample size and choosing an appropriate econometric technique to address the main 
hypothesis.
Moreover, previous literature is mainly  concentrated in the US market and less focus has been  
placed on the Swedish market. This makes it difficult t o compare results a cross jurisdictions 
as there might be changes in market microstructure and regulations in different countries. For 
instance, the United States and the European Union have different legislations for spoofing.
In addition to this, researchers Lee et al. (2009), Tao et al. (2022), Wang (2015), and Wang et al. 
(2021) state the main problem that arises while designing experiments to quantify spoofing is that 
it is hard to gain access to transaction-level data that includes order IDs or broker IDS. Therefore 
it is not possible to identify if the same trader placed the entry and the exit order. The observed 
sample of this paper does not include order IDs. Underfitting and data constraints might reduce 
the predictive power of the results of the logistic regression model. Therefore, it is difficult 
to comprehensively decompose the whole dynamic of a typical spoofing window.
31

6 Conclusion
This
thesis examines the spoofing puzzle posed by ex-ante spoofing in three ways. Firstly, by
identifying Unbalanced Quoting (UQ) to be the most suitable metric that can detect potential
spoofing in the limit order book of OMXS30 futures contracts. Our observed sample consists of
intraday LOB data and ranges from May to June 2019. Secondly, by studying the two phases,
namely phase 1 (Order Entry) and phase 2 (Order Cancellation) of a spoofing window, we are
able to identify the time difference which is one second between the two phases. Lastly, we
use contingency table and logistic regression to demonstrate the relationship between spoofing
proxiesРђЊHighQuotingActivity(HQA),UnbalancedQuoting(UQ),andAbnormalCancellations
(AC), and changes in the LOB mid-price (MPC).Based on the results from the contingency table
approachAC,UQandHQAaresuitableproxiesforpotentialspoofingastheyareassociatedwith
MPC. However, UQ comes out as the strongest proxy for detecting potential spoofing and AC
as the weakest indicator. Our findings from the logistic model reveal the impact of spoofing on
price discovery. UQ impacts the price negatively, whereas HQA impacts it positively. However,
when used jointly with UQ, even HQA impacts the mid-price negatively. For, the relationship
between AC and the impact on price discovery, our analysis yields a statistically insignificant
result.
Spoofing induces market failure as there is a disruption of the РђЎinvisible handРђЎ and thus leads to
the creation of deadweight loss for the uninformed trader and the formation of an exploitative
profit motive for an informed trader. The deviation of price from its fundamental value has both
microeconomic and macroeconomic consequences. The rise in opacity and the recent disclosure
change at Nasdaq Nordic spoof traders might find it easier to conceal spoof orders. Furthermore,
manipulation in one market can spill over to other markets(cross-market spoofing) and may lead
to a collapse of the broader financial system. Hence, there needs to be suitable regulatory checks
in place to tackle spoofing. Market surveillance analysts should work in tandem with regulators
to correctly evaluate dubious intent indicative of spoofing.
One of the most salient contributions of this paper is its focus on ex-ante spoofing. Previous lit-
erature is mainly concentrated on ex-post spoofing and its detrimental impact on prices. Putni┼є┼А
(2011) recommends that researchers should focus on what happens before manipulation occurs
but researchers are usually discouraged by the complexity of the data. Secondly, we change the
nature of the metrics to identify the side of the LOB leveraged for spoofing. Do and Putni┼є┼А
(2023) take absolute values and do not assign a directional sign to their proposed metrics. By
studying the directional impact of spoofing proxies with changes in mid-price, we outline buy-
side spoofing is relatively preferred over sell-side spoofing. Moreover, previous literature only
demonstrates the association between spoofing proxies and mid-price, thus our analysis takes
it one step further by showcasing that mid-price. reacts negatively with order imbalance and
positively with order cancellations.
Additionally, there is a lack of empirical literature on spoofing which is a gap addressed in our
study. Furthermore, this paper delves into the Swedish market, a jurisdiction that has not been
popular in previous research on spoofing. The results can also offer interesting insight into the
Nordic market following the implementation of post-trade anonymity on Nasdaq Nordic. The
paper also gives valuable insights into the prosecuted spoofing cases in the Swedish market and
the results can be used as a basis to study other Nordic markets.
32

Based on our analysis, we would like to make a few recommendations for further research. First
and foremost ex-ante spoofing and the period preceding market manipulation should be given
due importance. Designing surveillance systems that could detect spoofing before it wreaks
havoc in the financial market should be a priority. Regulatory and compliance checks should
also occur at the firm level since institutional investors such as JP Morgan were accused of
spoofing. Secondly, the data can be divided into a training set (non-manipulated cases) and a
test set (manipulated) cases to assess the suitability of relevant metrics indicative of spoofing.
Thirdly, machine learning models and neural networks could be used to detect ex-ante spoofing
(Cao et al., 2014; Putni┼є┼А, 2011). Finally, the price impact of spoofing in different phases can
be explained more quantitatively by introducing market quality measures such as volatility and
liquidity into the dataset. Furthermore, instead of just focusing on order book imbalance and
order cancellations researchers can also look at order-to-trade ratios to observe unusual patterns
in LOB order flow. Order-to-trade ratios measure the discrepancy between quote orders entered,
modified, or cancelled and executed trades. An interesting angle for future research could also
be to study the impact of dark pools and market opacity on spoofing to investigate if lack of
market transparency encourages spoofing.

Deep Learning in Quantitative Trading
Elements in Quantitative Finance
DOI: 10.1017/9781009707091
First published online: September 2025
Zihao Zhang
University of Oxford
Stefan Zohren
University of Oxford
Author for correspondence: Zihao Zhang, zhangzihao@hotmail.co.uk
Abstract: This Element provides a comprehensive guide to deep learning
in quantitative trading, merging foundational theory with hands-on
applications. It is organized into two parts. The first part introduces the
fundamentals of financial time-series and supervised learning, exploring
various network architectures, from feedforward to state-of-the-art. To
ensure robustness and mitigate overfitting on complex real-world data, a
complete workflow is presented, from initial data analysis to
cross-validation techniques tailored to financial data. Building on this, the
second part applies deep learning methods to a range of financial tasks.
The authors demonstrate how deep learning models can enhance both
time-series and cross-sectional momentum trading strategies, generate
predictive signals, and be formulated as an end-to-end framework for
portfolio optimization. Applications include a mixture of data from daily
data to high-frequency microstructure data for a variety of asset classes.
Throughout, they include illustrative code examples and provide a
dedicated GitHub repository with detailed implementations.
Keywords: deep learning, machine learning, reinforcement learning,
time-series, neural networks, quantitative trading, portfolio optimization,
market microstructure, momentum trading, volatility scalings
JEL classifications: A12, B34, C56, D78, E90

have collaborated with a common goal: to optimize financial returns while
minimizing the inherent risks of trading. This shared ambition has fueled
the evolution of quantitative trading strategies, which harness the power of
mathematicalandcomputationalmodelstoanalyzeandinterpretfinancialdata.
Traditionally, statistical time-series models have served as the cornerstone
of predictive signal generation in quantitative trading. These models, such as
ARIMA and GARCH, have proven effective in capturing trends and volatil-
ity in financial time-series data. However, such models are often constrained
by their linear nature and the stringent assumptions, such as stationarity and
normality, that they impose upon the data. Given the inherently complex and
nonlinearbehavioroffinancialmarkets,theselimitationscanleadtosubopti-
malperformance,particularlyindynamicandunpredictablemarketconditions.
To address these challenges, practitioners have historically relied upon man-
ually crafted features to enhance the predictive power of their models. By
engineering features that capture specific market dynamics, such as momen-
tum, mean reversion, and volatility clusters, researchers aim to approximate
theunderlyingcomplexityoffinancialsystems.However,thisprocessislabor-
intensive, requiring significant domain expertise and time. Moreover, manual
feature engineering is susceptible to human bias, potentially introducing or
overlookingcriticalpatternsorrelationshipsinthedata.
The increasing demand for more robust and scalable solutions has under-
scoredtheneedforadvancedmethodologiescapableofidentifyingandlever-
agingnonlinearrelationshipswithinfinancialdata.Deeplearning,aspecialized
branch of machine learning, utilizes multi-layered neural networks to auton-
omously learn and uncover meaningful patterns within large and complex
datasets. The core advantage of deep learning is its capacity to learn hierar-
chical representations of data. By progressively extracting features from raw
inputs, deep learning models are capable of capturing complex relationships
andsubtlepatternsthattraditionalstatisticalmethodsoftenfailtodetect.These
capabilities make them especially well suited for addressing the complexities
offinancialmarkets,whicharecharacterizedbyhighvolatility,intricateinter-
dependencies,andnoisydata.Specifically,deeplearningoffersseveraldistinct
advantages:Itcanhandlebothstructuredandunstructureddata,suchasnews
articlesandsocialmediasentiment;itcanadapttochangingmarketconditions
and regimes; it can uncover complex patterns as more complex data increas-
inglyrequiresmorecomplexmodelingtechniques;itcanbeusedforarangeof
strategytypes,fromhigh-frequencyexecutionproblemstolong-termportfolios
optimization.
This Element delves deeply into the transformative role of deep learn-
ing in modern quantitative trading, offering a thorough examination of how

bution and stationarity), and cross-validation techniques tailored specifically
for financial data. These steps are critical for building models that are robust
andreliable.
In the second part of this Element, we focus on applying deep learning
algorithms to various financial problems. One of the most fundamental tasks
in quantitative trading is generating predictive signals. We explore various
deep learning architectures for this purpose, showcasing how these networks
can be leveraged to predict market movements. Building on this foundation,
we delve into more advanced frameworks where deep networks are adopted
to enhance time-series momentum and cross-sectional momentum trading
strategies. Further, we discuss portfolio optimization and present methods to

2.5 Serial Covariance, Correlation, and Stationarity
For time-series forecasting, we make predictions based on historical obser-
vations from previous time stamps. While increments in financial time-series
are close to independent random variables, and are indeed often modeled by
stochasticprocessessuchasGeometricBrownianMotion(GBM),itisstillpos-
sible to identify and exploit small dependencies to make predictions. We can
measure such dependence with serial covariances (autocovariances) or serial
correlations(autocorrelations).
Serialcovariancereferstothemeasureofhowtwovariableschangetogether
over time within a time-series. In the context of financial time-series, it spe-
cifically measures the covariance between different observations of the same
financialvariableatdifferentpointsintime.Intuitively,autocovariancetellsus
howtwoinstancesofatime-series (xt,xs)atdifferenttimepointsmovetogether.
Understandingserialcovarianceisbeneficialforidentifyingpatternsandpre-
dicting future values based on historical data. For example, if the price of a
stocktodayispositivelycorrelatedwithitspriceyesterday,thismightindicate
afutureupwardtrend.
Correlation quantifies both the magnitude and the orientation of the linear
association between two time-series. In contrast to covariance, correlation is

Next, we discuss another concept that is commonly used in time-series
known as stationarity. Stationarity refers to the statistical property of a time-

In financial markets, where data often exhibit complex patterns, achieving

Therearetwoformsofstationarity:strictstationarityandweakstationarity.

very restrictive and very few real-world examples meet this requirement.

AlphaassessesaninvestmentРђЎsperformancerelativetoabenchmarkindex.

egy contributes over what is anticipated based on the assetРђЎs systematic risk.

3.3 Convolutional Neural Networks
Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) constitute a class of deep learning
architectures meticulously engineered to process images and other structured
grid-like data. These networks have fundamentally changed the landscape of
computer vision, allowing machines to carry out tasks such as image recog-
nition,objectdetection,andimagesegmentationwithperformancelevelsthat
rival human capabilities. Drawing inspiration from the visual cortex of ani-
mals, the architecture of CNNs enables them to automatically and adaptively
learn spatial hierarchies of features from input data, thereby enhancing their
effectivenessinvariouspatternrecognitiontasks.
CNNs are arguably the most important network structures as they inspired
much of the development of modern deep learning algorithms over the past
decade through initial breakthroughs in performance on image recognition
problems. Marking a pivotal breakthrough in computer vision, Krizhevsky,

3.7.3 Transformers-Based Time-Series Models
Theusageoftransformersfortime-seriesanalysishasattractedgreatpopular-
ity.Becausetime-seriesproblemscomewiththeirownuniquechallenges,such
as temporal dependencies, irregular sampling, and varying sequence lengths,
there has been a surge of research exploring how to tailor transformers spe-
cifically for these use cases (see Y.Wangetal. (2024) for a recent review).
Broadly, one can categorize transformers designed for time-series tasks into
twomaingroups:onebasedontheapplicationdomain,andanotherbasedon
the underlying network architecture. Figure 16shows the groupings of these
modelsandtheirrespectivesub-fields.
Fromanapplication-domainperspective,time-seriestransformersaretypi-
cally tailored toward four major tasks: forecasting, imputation, classification,
and anomaly detection. Our main focus here is on forecasting, but we briefly
mentionalltasksforcompleteness.Forecastinginvolvespredictingfutureval-
ues based on historical data patterns. Transformers excel here by capturing
long-range temporal dependencies that classical models might miss. Imputa-
tion deals with filling in missing or corrupted data points. The self-attention
mechanism helps the model learn relationships across different time steps
to recover lost information. For the classification of sequences, transformers
can leverage learned feature embeddings to distinguish between categories
or labels associated with entire sequences or specific time intervals. Finally,
